[Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan")'s tenure as the [40th president of the United States](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States "List of presidents of the United States") began with [his first inauguration](/wiki/First_inauguration_of_Ronald_Reagan "First inauguration of Ronald Reagan") on January 20, 1981, and ended on January 20, 1989. Reagan, a [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") from [California](/wiki/California "California"), took office after defeating the [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") incumbent president [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") and [independent](/wiki/Independent_politicians_in_the_United_States "Independent politicians in the United States") congressman [John B. Anderson](/wiki/John_B._Anderson "John B. Anderson") in the [1980 presidential election](/wiki/1980_United_States_presidential_election "1980 United States presidential election"). Four years later, he won re-election in the [1984 presidential election](/wiki/1984_United_States_presidential_election "1984 United States presidential election"), after defeating the Democratic nominee [Walter Mondale](/wiki/Walter_Mondale "Walter Mondale"). Reagan was constitutionally limited to two terms and was succeeded by his vice president, [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush"), who won the [1988 presidential election](/wiki/1988_United_States_presidential_election "1988 United States presidential election"). Reagan's 1980 landslide election resulted from a [conservative shift to the right](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") in American politics, including a loss of confidence in [liberal](/wiki/Modern_liberalism_in_the_United_States "Modern liberalism in the United States"), [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal"), and [Great Society](/wiki/Great_Society "Great Society") programs and priorities that had dominated the national agenda since the 1930s.

Domestically, the Reagan administration enacted a [major tax cut](/wiki/Economic_Recovery_Tax_Act_of_1981 "Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981"), sought to cut non-military spending, and eliminated federal regulations. The administration's economic policies, known as "[Reaganomics](/wiki/Reaganomics "Reaganomics")", were inspired by [supply-side economics](/wiki/Supply-side_economics "Supply-side economics"). The combination of tax cuts and an increase in defense spending led to budget deficits, and the [federal debt](/wiki/National_debt_of_the_United_States "National debt of the United States") increased significantly during Reagan's tenure. Reagan signed the [Tax Reform Act of 1986](/wiki/Tax_Reform_Act_of_1986 "Tax Reform Act of 1986"), simplifying the tax code by reducing rates and removing several tax breaks, and the [Immigration Reform and Control Act of 1986](/wiki/Immigration_Reform_and_Control_Act_of_1986 "Immigration Reform and Control Act of 1986"), which enacted sweeping changes to U.S. immigration law and granted amnesty to three million [illegal immigrants](/wiki/Illegal_immigration_to_the_United_States "Illegal immigration to the United States"). Reagan also appointed more [federal judges](/wiki/Federal_judiciary_of_the_United_States "Federal judiciary of the United States") than any other president, including four [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") Justices.

Reagan's [foreign policy](/wiki/Foreign_policy_of_the_Reagan_administration "Foreign policy of the Reagan administration") stance was resolutely [anti-communist](/wiki/Anti-communist "Anti-communist"). Its plan of action, known as the [Reagan Doctrine](/wiki/Reagan_Doctrine "Reagan Doctrine"), sought to [roll back](/wiki/Rollback "Rollback") the global influence of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") in an attempt to end the Cold War. Under his doctrine, the Reagan administration initiated a massive buildup of the United States military; promoted new technologies such as missile defense systems; and in 1983 undertook an [invasion of Grenada](/wiki/United_States_invasion_of_Grenada "United States invasion of Grenada"), the first major overseas action by U.S. troops since the end of the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"). The administration also created controversy by granting [aid to paramilitary forces](/wiki/United_States_and_state-sponsored_terrorism "United States and state-sponsored terrorism") seeking to overthrow leftist governments, particularly in war-torn Central America and [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"). Specifically, the Reagan administration engaged in covert arms sales to [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") to fund [Contra](/wiki/Contras "Contras") rebels in Nicaragua that were fighting to overthrow their nation's socialist government. The resulting [Iran–Contra affair](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Contra_affair "Iran–Contra affair") led to the conviction or resignation of several administration officials. During Reagan's second term, he worked with Soviet leader [Mikhail Gorbachev](/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev "Mikhail Gorbachev") to sign a [major arms control agreement](/wiki/Intermediate-Range_Nuclear_Forces_Treaty "Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty"). In 1986, Congress overrode Reagan's veto of [a bill](/wiki/Comprehensive_Anti-Apartheid_Act "Comprehensive Anti-Apartheid Act") intended to implement economic sanctions against South Africa's [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") regime.

Historians and political scientists generally [rank](/wiki/Historical_rankings_of_presidents_of_the_United_States "Historical rankings of presidents of the United States") Reagan in the upper tier of American presidents, and consider him to be one of the most important presidents since [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt"). Supporters of Reagan's presidency have pointed to his contributions to the economic recovery of the 1980s, the peaceful end of the Cold War, and a broader restoration of American confidence. Reagan's presidency has also received criticism for rising budget deficits and [wealth inequality](/wiki/Wealth_inequality_in_the_United_States "Wealth inequality in the United States") during and after his presidency. Due to Reagan's popularity with the public and advocacy of American conservatism, some historians have described the period during and after his presidency as the [Reagan era](/wiki/Reagan_era "Reagan era").

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

### Conservative shift in politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Conservative shift in politics")]

Further information: [Conservatism in the United States](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") and [Reagan era](/wiki/Reagan_era "Reagan era")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Ronald_Reagan_with_cowboy_hat_12-0071M_edit.jpg/250px-Ronald_Reagan_with_cowboy_hat_12-0071M_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ronald_Reagan_with_cowboy_hat_12-0071M_edit.jpg)

Ronald Reagan with a cowboy hat at Rancho Del Cielo.

Even prior to becoming president, Reagan was the leader of a dramatic conservative shift that undercut many of the domestic and foreign policies that had dominated the national agenda for decades.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) A major factor in the rise of conservatism was the growing distrust of government in the aftermath of the [Watergate scandal](/wiki/Watergate_scandal "Watergate scandal"). While distrust of high officials had been an American characteristic for two centuries, Watergate engendered heightened levels of suspicion and encouraged the media to engage in a vigorous search for scandals.[[3]](#cite_note-3) An unexpected new factor was the emergence of the [religious right](/wiki/Christian_right "Christian right") as a cohesive political force that gave strong support to conservatism.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Other factors in the rise of the conservative movement were the emergence of a "[culture war](/wiki/Culture_war "Culture war")" as a triangular battle among conservatives, traditional liberals, and the [New Left](/wiki/New_Left "New Left"), involving such issues as individual freedom, divorce, sexual freedom, abortion, and homosexuality.[[6]](#cite_note-6) A mass movement of population from the cities to the suburbs led to the creation of a new group of voters less attached to [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") economic policies and [machine politics](/wiki/Political_machine "Political machine").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz200823–24-7) Meanwhile, it became socially acceptable for conservative Southern whites, especially well-educated suburbanites, to vote Republican. Though the civil rights legislation of the 1960s had been a triumphal issue for liberalism and had created a new, pro-Democratic black electorate, it had also destroyed the argument that whites had to vote Democratic to protect segregation in the South.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Responding to these various trends, Reagan and other conservatives successfully presented conservative ideas as an alternative to a public that had grown disillusioned with New Deal liberalism and the Democratic Party.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz20084–7-9) Reagan's charisma and speaking skills helped him frame conservatism as an optimistic, forward-looking vision for the country.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz2008137–138-10)

### 1980 election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1980 election")]

Main articles: [Ronald Reagan 1980 presidential campaign](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_1980_presidential_campaign "Ronald Reagan 1980 presidential campaign"), [1980 United States presidential election](/wiki/1980_United_States_presidential_election "1980 United States presidential election"), and [Presidential transition of Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Presidential_transition_of_Ronald_Reagan "Presidential transition of Ronald Reagan")

Further information: [1980 United States elections](/wiki/1980_United_States_elections "1980 United States elections"), [1980 Republican Party presidential primaries](/wiki/1980_Republican_Party_presidential_primaries "1980 Republican Party presidential primaries"), and [1980 Republican National Convention](/wiki/1980_Republican_National_Convention "1980 Republican National Convention")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/ElectoralCollege1980.svg/250px-ElectoralCollege1980.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ElectoralCollege1980.svg)

1980 Electoral College vote results

Reagan, who had served as [Governor of California](/wiki/Governor_of_California "Governor of California") from 1967 to 1975, narrowly lost the [1976 Republican presidential primaries](/wiki/1976_Republican_Party_presidential_primaries "1976 Republican Party presidential primaries") to incumbent president [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford"). With the defeat of Ford by Democrat [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") in the [1976 election](/wiki/1976_United_States_presidential_election "1976 United States presidential election"), Reagan immediately became the front-runner for the 1980 Republican presidential nomination.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeisberg201656–57-11) A darling of the conservative movement, Reagan faced more moderate Republicans such as [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush"), [Howard Baker](/wiki/Howard_Baker "Howard Baker"), and [Bob Dole](/wiki/Bob_Dole "Bob Dole") in the [1980 Republican presidential primaries](/wiki/1980_Republican_Party_presidential_primaries "1980 Republican Party presidential primaries"). After Bush won the Iowa caucuses, he became Reagan's primary challenger, but Reagan won the New Hampshire primary and most of the following primaries, gaining an insurmountable delegate lead by the end of March 1980. Ford was Reagan's first choice for his running mate, but Reagan backed away from the idea out of the fear of a "co-presidency" in which Ford would exercise an unusual degree of power. Reagan instead chose Bush, and the Reagan-Bush ticket was nominated at the [1980 Republican National Convention](/wiki/1980_Republican_National_Convention "1980 Republican National Convention"). Meanwhile, Carter won the Democratic nomination, defeating a primary challenge by Senator [Ted Kennedy](/wiki/Ted_Kennedy "Ted Kennedy"). Polls taken after the party conventions showed a tied race between Reagan and Carter, while independent candidate [John B. Anderson](/wiki/John_B._Anderson "John B. Anderson") had the support of many moderates.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeisberg201661–63-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/President_Jimmy_Carter_welcomes_President-elect_Ronal_Reagan_and_Nancy_Reagan_to_the_White_House_for_a_tour.jpg/250px-President_Jimmy_Carter_welcomes_President-elect_Ronal_Reagan_and_Nancy_Reagan_to_the_White_House_for_a_tour.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Jimmy_Carter_welcomes_President-elect_Ronal_Reagan_and_Nancy_Reagan_to_the_White_House_for_a_tour.jpg)

Outgoing president [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") and President-elect Ronald Reagan with his wife [Nancy](/wiki/Nancy_Reagan "Nancy Reagan") in the [Oval Office](/wiki/Oval_Office "Oval Office") on November 20, 1980

The 1980 general campaign between Reagan and Carter was conducted amid a multitude of domestic concerns and the ongoing [Iran hostage crisis](/wiki/Iran_hostage_crisis "Iran hostage crisis"). After winning the Republican nomination, Reagan pivoted to the center. Though he continued to champion a major tax cut, Reagan backed off from his support for free trade and the privatization of [Social Security](/wiki/Social_Security_(United_States) "Social Security (United States)"), and promised to consider [arms control](/wiki/Arms_control "Arms control") treaties with the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"). He instead sought to focus the race on Carter's handling of the economy. Mired with an approval rating in the low 30s, Carter also waged a negative campaign, focusing on the supposed risk of war if Reagan took office.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201523–27-13)

Reagan and Carter met in one presidential debate, held just one week before election day. Reagan delivered an effective performance, asking voters, "Are you better off today than you were four years ago?"[[14]](#cite_note-14) In response to a characterization by Carter of his record regarding Medicare, Reagan replied with a phrase that helped define the election and endure in the political lexicon: "[There you go again](/wiki/There_you_go_again "There you go again")."

Though the race had been widely regarded as a close contest, Reagan won over the large majority of undecided voters.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201527–28-15) Reagan took 50.7% of the popular vote and 489 of the 538 electoral votes. Carter won 41% of the popular vote and 49 electoral votes, while Anderson won 6.6% of the popular vote. In the concurrent [congressional elections](/wiki/1980_United_States_elections "1980 United States elections"), Republicans took control of the [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") for the first time since the 1950s, while Democrats retained control of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005149–151-16)

Reagan tapped [James Baker](/wiki/James_Baker "James Baker"), who had run Bush's 1980 campaign, as his first [chief of staff](/wiki/White_House_Chief_of_Staff "White House Chief of Staff"). Baker, [Deputy Chief of Staff](/wiki/White_House_Deputy_Chief_of_Staff "White House Deputy Chief of Staff") [Michael Deaver](/wiki/Michael_Deaver "Michael Deaver"), and [Counselor](/wiki/Counselor_to_the_President "Counselor to the President") [Edwin Meese](/wiki/Edwin_Meese "Edwin Meese") formed the "troika", the key White House staffers early in Reagan's presidency.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015241–246-17) Baker quickly established himself as the most powerful member of the troika and the overseer of day-to-day operations, while Meese had nominal leadership of policy development and Deaver orchestrated Reagan's public appearances.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201551–52-18) Aside from the troika, other important White House staffers included [Richard Darman](/wiki/Richard_Darman "Richard Darman") and [David Gergen](/wiki/David_Gergen "David Gergen").[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201552,-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Reagan chose [Alexander Haig](/wiki/Alexander_Haig "Alexander Haig"), a former general who had served as chief of staff to [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon"), as his first secretary of state. Other major Cabinet appointees included Secretary of Defense [Caspar Weinberger](/wiki/Caspar_Weinberger "Caspar Weinberger"), a former Nixon cabinet official who would preside over an increase in defense spending, and Secretary of the Treasury [Donald Regan](/wiki/Donald_Regan "Donald Regan"), a bank executive. Reagan selected [David Stockman](/wiki/David_Stockman "David Stockman"), a young congressman from [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), as the director of the [Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/Office_of_Management_and_Budget "Office of Management and Budget").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015246–248-22) [CIA director](/wiki/Director_of_the_Central_Intelligence_Agency "Director of the Central Intelligence Agency") [William J. Casey](/wiki/William_J._Casey "William J. Casey") emerged as an important figure in the administration, as the CIA would figure prominently into Reagan's Cold War initiatives. Reagan downgraded the importance of the [national security advisor](/wiki/National_Security_Advisor_(United_States) "National Security Advisor (United States)"), and six different individuals held that position during Reagan's presidency.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008864–866-23)

Haig left the cabinet in 1982 after clashing with other members of the Reagan administration, and was replaced by another former Nixon administration official, [George P. Shultz](/wiki/George_P._Shultz "George P. Shultz").[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015376–381-24) By 1982, National Security Advisor [William P. Clark Jr.](/wiki/William_P._Clark_Jr. "William P. Clark Jr."), [Ambassador to the United Nations](/wiki/United_States_Ambassador_to_the_United_Nations "United States Ambassador to the United Nations") [Jeane Kirkpatrick](/wiki/Jeane_Kirkpatrick "Jeane Kirkpatrick"), and CIA Director Casey had established themselves as the major figures in the formulation of the administration's foreign policy.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201581–82-25) Shultz eventually emerged as the administration's most influential foreign policy figure, moving the administration towards a less confrontational policy with the Soviet Union.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015103–104-26)

Baker and Treasury Secretary Regan switched positions at the beginning of Reagan's second term.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015472–474-27) Regan centralized power within his office, and he took on the responsibilities that had been held by Baker, Deaver, and Meese, the latter of whom succeeded [William French Smith](/wiki/William_French_Smith "William French Smith") as attorney general in 1985.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz2008178–180-28) Regan frequently clashed with First Lady [Nancy Reagan](/wiki/Nancy_Reagan "Nancy Reagan"), and he left the administration in the wake of the [Iran–Contra affair](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Contra_affair "Iran–Contra affair") and Republican losses in the [1986 mid-term elections](/wiki/1986_United_States_elections "1986 United States elections"). Regan was replaced by former Senate Majority Leader [Howard Baker](/wiki/Howard_Baker "Howard Baker").[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015645–649-29)

## Judicial appointments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Judicial appointments")]

### Supreme Court

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Supreme Court")]

Main article: [Ronald Reagan Supreme Court candidates](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_Supreme_Court_candidates "Ronald Reagan Supreme Court candidates")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/William_Rehnquist.jpg/250px-William_Rehnquist.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Rehnquist.jpg)

Reagan appointed [William Rehnquist](/wiki/William_Rehnquist "William Rehnquist") to the office of [chief justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States") in 1986; he served until his death in 2005

Reagan made four successful appointments to the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") during his eight years in office. In 1981, he successfully nominated [Sandra Day O'Connor](/wiki/Sandra_Day_O%27Connor "Sandra Day O'Connor") to succeed [Associate Justice](/wiki/Associate_Justice_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Associate Justice of the Supreme Court of the United States") [Potter Stewart](/wiki/Potter_Stewart "Potter Stewart"), fulfilling a campaign promise to name the first woman to the Supreme Court. Democrats, who had planned to vigorously oppose Reagan's nominations to the Supreme Court, approved of the nomination of O'Connor. However, the [Christian right](/wiki/Christian_right "Christian right") was astonished and dismayed with O'Connor, who they feared would not overturn the Supreme Court's decision in *[Roe v. Wade](/wiki/Roe_v._Wade "Roe v. Wade")*, which had established [abortion](/wiki/Abortion_in_the_United_States "Abortion in the United States") as protected from government interference via the Constitution.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz2008189–190-31) O'Connor served on the Supreme Court until 2006, and was generally considered to be a centrist conservative.[[32]](#cite_note-bisk-32)

In 1986, Reagan elevated Associate Justice [William Rehnquist](/wiki/William_Rehnquist "William Rehnquist") to the position of [Chief Justice of the United States](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States") after [Warren Burger](/wiki/Warren_Burger "Warren Burger") chose to retire.[[33]](#cite_note-SCOTUS-33) Rehnquist, a member of the conservative wing of the court,[[32]](#cite_note-bisk-32) was the third sitting associate justice to be elevated to chief justice, after [Edward Douglass White](/wiki/Edward_Douglass_White "Edward Douglass White") and [Harlan F. Stone](/wiki/Harlan_F._Stone "Harlan F. Stone"). Reagan successfully nominated [Antonin Scalia](/wiki/Antonin_Scalia "Antonin Scalia") to fill Rehnquist's position as an associate justice of the Supreme Court.[[33]](#cite_note-SCOTUS-33) Scalia became a member of the court's conservative wing.[[32]](#cite_note-bisk-32)

Reagan faced greater difficulties in filling the final Supreme Court vacancy, which arose due to the retirement of [Lewis F. Powell Jr.](/wiki/Lewis_F._Powell_Jr. "Lewis F. Powell Jr.") Reagan nominated [Robert Bork](/wiki/Robert_Bork "Robert Bork") in July 1987, but the nomination was [rejected by the Senate](/wiki/Robert_Bork_Supreme_Court_nomination "Robert Bork Supreme Court nomination") in October 1987.[[33]](#cite_note-SCOTUS-33) Later that month, Reagan announced the nomination of [Douglas H. Ginsburg](/wiki/Douglas_H._Ginsburg "Douglas H. Ginsburg"), but Ginsburg withdrew from consideration in November 1987. Finally, Reagan nominated [Anthony Kennedy](/wiki/Anthony_Kennedy "Anthony Kennedy"), who won Senate confirmation in February 1988.[[33]](#cite_note-SCOTUS-33) Along with O'Connor, Kennedy served as the key swing vote on the Supreme Court in the decades after Reagan left office.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Other courts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Other courts")]

Further information: [List of federal judges appointed by Ronald Reagan](/wiki/List_of_federal_judges_appointed_by_Ronald_Reagan "List of federal judges appointed by Ronald Reagan") and [Ronald Reagan judicial appointment controversies](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_judicial_appointment_controversies "Ronald Reagan judicial appointment controversies")

Reagan appointed a combined total of 368 judges to the [United States courts of appeals](/wiki/United_States_courts_of_appeals "United States courts of appeals") and the [United States district courts](/wiki/United_States_district_court "United States district court"), [more than any other president](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States_by_judicial_appointments "List of presidents of the United States by judicial appointments"). The vast majority of his judicial appointees were conservative, and many of the appointees were affiliated with the conservative [Federalist Society](/wiki/Federalist_Society "Federalist Society").[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeisberg2016116–117-35) Partly because Congress passed a law creating new federal judicial positions in 1984, Reagan had appointed nearly half of the federal judiciary by the time he left office in 1989.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015178-36)

## Assassination attempt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Assassination attempt")]

Main article: [Attempted assassination of Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Attempted_assassination_of_Ronald_Reagan "Attempted assassination of Ronald Reagan")

[![Ronald Reagan waves his hand as he walks out of the Washington Hilton. Surrounding him are secret service agents, policemen, press secretary James Brady, and aide Michael Deaver.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/President_Ronald_Reagan_moments_before_he_was_shot_in_an_assassination_attempt_1981.jpg/250px-President_Ronald_Reagan_moments_before_he_was_shot_in_an_assassination_attempt_1981.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Ronald_Reagan_moments_before_he_was_shot_in_an_assassination_attempt_1981.jpg)

Reagan (center) waves just before he is shot on March 30, 1981

On March 30, 1981, only 69 days into the new administration, Reagan, his press secretary [James Brady](/wiki/James_Brady "James Brady"), Washington police officer [Thomas Delahanty](/wiki/Thomas_Delahanty "Thomas Delahanty"), and Secret Service agent [Tim McCarthy](/wiki/Tim_McCarthy "Tim McCarthy") were struck by gunfire from would-be assassin [John Hinckley Jr.](/wiki/John_Hinckley_Jr. "John Hinckley Jr.") outside the [Washington Hilton Hotel](/wiki/Hilton_Washington "Hilton Washington"). Although Reagan was initially reported to be "close to death" upon arrival at [George Washington University Hospital](/wiki/George_Washington_University_Hospital "George Washington University Hospital"), he underwent surgery and recovered quickly from a broken rib, a punctured lung, and [internal bleeding](/wiki/Internal_bleeding "Internal bleeding").[[37]](#cite_note-cnn_transcript-37) Reagan was released from the hospital on April 11, becoming the first serving president to survive being wounded in an assassination attempt.[[38]](#cite_note-38) The failed assassination attempt had great influence on Reagan's popularity; polls indicated his approval rating to be around 73%.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeuchtenburg2015597–598-40) Many pundits and journalists later described the failed assassination as a critical moment in Reagan's presidency, as his newfound popularity provided critical momentum in passing his domestic agenda.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeuchtenburg2015598–599-41)

## Domestic affairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Domestic affairs")]

Main article: [Domestic policy of the Reagan administration](/wiki/Domestic_policy_of_the_Reagan_administration "Domestic policy of the Reagan administration")

Reagan used his White House staff to shape major domestic policies. His chief of staff made heavy use of the Office of Policy Development in supervising cabinet action on the Reagan initiatives.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

### "Reaganomics" and taxation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: \"Reaganomics\" and taxation")]

Main article: [Reaganomics](/wiki/Reaganomics "Reaganomics")

#### Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981")]

Reagan implemented [neoliberal](/wiki/Neoliberal "Neoliberal") economic policies based on [supply-side economics](/wiki/Supply-side_economics "Supply-side economics"), advocating a *[laissez-faire](/wiki/Laissez-faire "Laissez-faire")* philosophy and [free-market](/wiki/Free_market_economy "Free market economy") fiscal policy.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) Reagan's taxation policies resembled those instituted by President [Calvin Coolidge](/wiki/Presidency_of_Calvin_Coolidge "Presidency of Calvin Coolidge") and Treasury Secretary [Andrew Mellon](/wiki/Andrew_Mellon "Andrew Mellon") in the 1920s. Reagan's team was also strongly influenced by contemporary economists such as [Arthur Laffer](/wiki/Arthur_Laffer "Arthur Laffer"), who rejected the then-dominant views of [Keynesian economists](/wiki/Keynesian_economics "Keynesian economics").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeuchtenburg2015595–596-45) Reagan relied on Laffer and other economists to argue that tax cuts would reduce inflation, which went against the prevailing Keynesian view.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015271–272-46) Supply-side advocates also asserted that cutting taxes would ultimately lead to higher government revenue due to economic growth, a proposition that was challenged by many economists.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005154–155-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/President_Ronald_Reagan_addresses_the_nation_from_the_Oval_Office_on_tax_reduction_legislation.jpg/250px-President_Ronald_Reagan_addresses_the_nation_from_the_Oval_Office_on_tax_reduction_legislation.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Ronald_Reagan_addresses_the_nation_from_the_Oval_Office_on_tax_reduction_legislation.jpg)

Outlining his plan for tax reduction legislation from the [Oval Office](/wiki/Oval_Office "Oval Office") in a televised address, July 1981

Republican congressman [Jack Kemp](/wiki/Jack_Kemp "Jack Kemp") and Republican senator [William Roth](/wiki/William_Roth "William Roth") had nearly won passage of a major tax cut during Carter's presidency, but Carter had prevented passage of the bill due to concerns about the deficit.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201520-48) Reagan made passage of the Kemp–Roth bill his top domestic priority upon taking office. As Democrats controlled the House of Representatives, passage of any bill would require the support of some House Democrats in addition to the support of congressional Republicans.[[49]](#cite_note-leuch599601-49) Reagan's victory in the 1980 presidential campaign had united Republicans around his leadership, while conservative Democrats like [Phil Gramm](/wiki/Phil_Gramm "Phil Gramm") of Texas (who later became a Republican) were eager to back some of Reagan's conservative policies.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201548–49-50) Throughout 1981, Reagan frequently met with members of Congress, focusing especially on winning support from conservative Southern Democrats.[[49]](#cite_note-leuch599601-49) Reagan also benefited from a conservative majority in the House during his first two years as president, with an estimated 230 votes during the 97th Congress, although this changed after the Democratic gains in the 1982 election, with House control switching to liberals within the Democratic caucus.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

In July 1981, the Senate voted 89–11 in favor of the tax cut bill favored by Reagan, and the House subsequently approved the bill in a 238–195 vote.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201561–62-52) The [Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981](/wiki/Economic_Recovery_Tax_Act_of_1981 "Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981") cut the top marginal tax rate from 70% to 50%, lowered the [capital gains tax](/wiki/Capital_gains_tax_in_the_United_States "Capital gains tax in the United States") from 28% to 20%, more than tripled the amount of inherited money exempt from the [estate tax](/wiki/Estate_tax_in_the_United_States "Estate tax in the United States"), and cut the [corporate tax](/wiki/Corporate_tax_in_the_United_States "Corporate tax in the United States").[[49]](#cite_note-leuch599601-49)[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201561–62-52) Reagan's success in passing a major tax bill and cutting the federal budget was hailed as the "Reagan Revolution" by some reporters; one columnist wrote that the Reagan's legislative success represented the "most formidable domestic initiative any president has driven through since the [Hundred Days](/wiki/First_100_days_of_Franklin_D._Roosevelt%27s_presidency "First 100 days of Franklin D. Roosevelt's presidency") of [Franklin Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt")".[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005157-53)

#### Later tax acts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Later tax acts")]

Faced with concerns about the mounting federal debt, Reagan agreed to raise taxes, signing the [Tax Equity and Fiscal Responsibility Act of 1982](/wiki/Tax_Equity_and_Fiscal_Responsibility_Act_of_1982 "Tax Equity and Fiscal Responsibility Act of 1982") (TEFRA).[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015346–349-54) Many of Reagan's conservative supporters condemned TEFRA, but Reagan argued that his administration would be unable to win further budget cuts without the tax hike.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz2008148–149-55) Among other provisions, TEFRA doubled the federal cigarette tax and rescinded a portion of the corporate tax cuts from the 1981 tax bill.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201562–63-56) By 1983, the amount of federal tax had fallen for all or almost all American taxpayers, but most strongly affected the wealthy; the proportion of income paid in taxes by the richest one percent fell from 29.8 percent to 24.8 percent.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201563-57) Partly due to the poor economy, Reagan's legislative momentum dissipated after his first year in office, and his party lost several seats in the House in the [1982 congressional elections](/wiki/United_States_elections,_1982 "United States elections, 1982").[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005162–163-58) Compared to other [midterm elections](/wiki/United_States_midterm_election "United States midterm election"), the losses were relatively small for the party holding the presidency, but conservative Democrats were less open to Reagan's initiatives after 1982.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201597–98,_164-59) As deficits continued to be an issue, Reagan signed another bill that raised taxes, the [Deficit Reduction Act of 1984](/wiki/Deficit_Reduction_Act_of_1984 "Deficit Reduction Act of 1984").[[60]](#cite_note-shapiro1-60)

With Donald Regan taking over as chief of staff in 1985, the Reagan administration made simplification of the tax code the central focus of its second term domestic agenda.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015540–541-61) Working with Speaker of the House [Tip O'Neill](/wiki/Tip_O%27Neill "Tip O'Neill"), a Democrat who also favored tax reform, Reagan overcame significant opposition from members of Congress in both parties to pass the [Tax Reform Act of 1986](/wiki/Tax_Reform_Act_of_1986 "Tax Reform Act of 1986").[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015542–544-62) The act simplified the tax code by reducing the number of tax brackets to four and slashing a number of tax breaks. The top rate was dropped to 28%, but capital gains taxes were increased on those with the highest incomes from 20% to 28%. The increase of the lowest tax bracket from 11% to 15% was more than offset by expansion of the personal exemption, [standard deduction](/wiki/Standard_deduction "Standard deduction"), and [earned income tax credit](/wiki/Earned_income_tax_credit "Earned income tax credit"). The net result was the removal of six million poor Americans from the income tax roll and a reduction of income tax liability at all income levels.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64) The net effect of Reagan's tax bills was that overall tax burden held steady at roughly 19 percent of [gross national product](/wiki/Gross_national_product "Gross national product").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005166-65)

### Government spending

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Government spending")]

Federal finances and GDP during Reagan's presidency[[66]](#cite_note-66)

| Fiscal Year | Receipts | Outlays | Surplus/ Deficit | [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") | Debt as a % of GDP[[67]](#cite_note-67) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1981 | 599.3 | 678.2 | −79.0 | 3,133.2 | 25.2 |
| 1982 | 617.8 | 745.7 | −128.0 | 3,313.4 | 27.9 |
| 1983 | 600.6 | 808.4 | −207.8 | 3,536.0 | 32.2 |
| 1984 | 666.4 | 851.8 | −185.4 | 3,949.2 | 33.1 |
| 1985 | 734.0 | 946.3 | −212.3 | 4,265.1 | 35.3 |
| 1986 | 769.2 | 990.4 | −221.2 | 4,526.3 | 38.5 |
| 1987 | 854.3 | 1,004.0 | −149.7 | 4,767.7 | 39.6 |
| 1988 | 909.2 | 1,064.4 | −155.2 | 5,138.6 | 39.9 |
| 1989 | 991.1 | 1,143.7 | −152.6 | 5,554.7 | 39.4 |
| Ref. | [[68]](#cite_note-68) | | | [[69]](#cite_note-69) | [[70]](#cite_note-70) |

Reagan prioritized tax cuts over spending cuts, arguing that lower revenue would eventually require lower spending.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015263–264-71) Nonetheless, Reagan was determined to decrease government spending and roll back or dismantle [Great Society](/wiki/Great_Society "Great Society") programs such as [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid") and the [Office of Economic Opportunity](/wiki/Office_of_Economic_Opportunity "Office of Economic Opportunity").[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeuchtenburg2015594–595-72) In August 1981, Reagan signed the Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act of 1981, which cut federal funding for social programs like [food stamps](/wiki/Food_Stamp_Program "Food Stamp Program"), [school lunch programs](/wiki/School_meal_programs_in_the_United_States "School meal programs in the United States"), and Medicaid.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201558–60-73) The [Comprehensive Employment and Training Act](/wiki/Comprehensive_Employment_and_Training_Act "Comprehensive Employment and Training Act"), which had provided for the employment of 300,000 workers in 1980, was also repealed,[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005157-53) and the administration tightened eligibility for [unemployment benefits](/wiki/Unemployment_benefits "Unemployment benefits").[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201585-74) Notably absent from the budget cuts was the [Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense"), which saw its budget bolstered.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015266–267-75)

Reagan experienced several legislative successes in his first year in office, but his attempts to cut federal domestic spending after 1981 met increasing congressional resistance.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeuchtenburg2015615–616-76) Spending on programs like [Supplemental Security Income](/wiki/Supplemental_Security_Income "Supplemental Security Income"), Medicaid, the [earned income tax credit](/wiki/Earned_income_tax_credit "Earned income tax credit"), and [Aid to Families with Dependent Children](/wiki/Aid_to_Families_with_Dependent_Children "Aid to Families with Dependent Children") all increased after 1982. The number of federal civilian employees rose during Reagan's tenure, from 2.9 million to 3.1 million.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005165-77) Reagan's policy of [New Federalism](/wiki/New_Federalism "New Federalism"), which sought to shift the responsibility for most social programs to state governments, found little support in Congress.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeuchtenburg2015618–619-78)

In 1981, OMB Director David Stockman won Reagan's approval to seek cuts to [Social Security](/wiki/Social_Security_(United_States) "Social Security (United States)") in 1981, but this plan was poorly received in Congress.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015300–303-79) In 1982, Reagan established the bipartisan [National Commission on Social Security Reform](/wiki/Greenspan_Commission "Greenspan Commission") to make recommendations to secure the long-term integrity of Social Security. The commission rejected Social Security privatization and other major changes to the program, but recommended expanding the Social Security base (by including exempt federal and nonprofit employees), raising Social Security taxes, and reducing some payments. These recommendations were enacted in the [Social Security Amendments of 1983](/wiki/History_of_Social_Security_in_the_United_States#1983_Amendments "History of Social Security in the United States"), which received bipartisan support.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015425–427-80) While Reagan avoided cuts to Social Security and [Medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(United_States) "Medicare (United States)") for most individuals,[[81]](#cite_note-81) his administration attempted to purge many people from the Social Security disability rolls.[[82]](#cite_note-82) Reagan's inability to implement major cuts to Social Security solidified its status as the "[third rail](/wiki/Third_rail_of_politics "Third rail of politics")" of U.S. politics, and future administrations would be reluctant to propose cuts to the popular program.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz2008149–150-83)

### Deficits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Deficits")]

As Reagan was unwilling to match his tax cuts with cuts to defense spending or Social Security, rising deficits became an issue.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeisberg201675–76-84) These deficits were exacerbated by the [early 1980s recession](/wiki/Early_1980s_recession "Early 1980s recession"), which cut into federal revenue.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeuchtenburg2015605–606-85) Unable to win further domestic spending cuts, and pressured to address the deficit, Reagan was forced to raise taxes after 1981.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015346–347-86) Nonetheless, the national debt more than tripled between fiscal year 1980 and fiscal year 1989, going from $914 billion to $2.7 trillion, while national debt as a percentage of GDP rose from 33 percent in 1981 to 53 percent in 1989. Reagan never submitted a balanced budget during his time in office.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005158–159-87)

In an effort to lower the national debt, Congress passed the [Gramm–Rudman–Hollings Balanced Budget Act](/wiki/Gramm%E2%80%93Rudman%E2%80%93Hollings_Balanced_Budget_Act "Gramm–Rudman–Hollings Balanced Budget Act"), which called for [automatic spending cuts](/wiki/Budget_sequestration "Budget sequestration") if Congress was unable to eliminate deficits through the regular budget-making process.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015178-36) However, Congress found ways around the automatic cuts and deficits continued to rise, ultimately leading to the passage of the [Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act of 1990](/wiki/Omnibus_Budget_Reconciliation_Act_of_1990 "Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act of 1990").[[88]](#cite_note-scline1-88)

### Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Economy")]

Reagan took office in the midst of poor economic conditions, as the country experienced [stagflation](/wiki/Stagflation "Stagflation"), a phenomenon in which both inflation and unemployment were high.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015221–222-89) The economy experienced a brief period of growth early in Reagan's first year in office, but plunged into a recession in July 1981.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201590–91-90) As the recession continued in the first two years of Reagan's presidency, many within Reagan's administration blamed the policies of [Paul Volcker](/wiki/Paul_Volcker "Paul Volcker"), the chair of the [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_System "Federal Reserve System"). But Reagan himself never criticized Volcker.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Volcker sought to fight inflation by pursuing a policy of "tight money" in which interest rates were set at a high level.[[92]](#cite_note-brands317319-92) High interest rates would restrict lending and investment, which would in turn lower inflation, raise unemployment and, at least in the short term, reduce economic growth.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201588–90-93) Unemployment reached a high of nearly 11% in 1982,[[92]](#cite_note-brands317319-92) [poverty](/wiki/Poverty_in_the_United_States "Poverty in the United States") rate rose from 11.7 percent to 15 percent.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201585-74) The country emerged from recession in 1983,[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015452–453-94) but not all shared equally in the economic recovery, and [economic inequality](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality") and the number of [homeless](/wiki/Homelessness_in_the_United_States "Homelessness in the United States") individuals both increased during the 1980s.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005166–167-95)[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015145-96) Fearful of damaging confidence in the economic recovery, Reagan nominated Volcker to a second term in 1983, and Volcker remained in office until 1987.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015668–671-97) Inflation dropped to approximately 3.5% in 1985, while the unemployment rate fell to about 5% in 1988.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005162–163-58) In 1987, Reagan appointed conservative economist [Alan Greenspan](/wiki/Alan_Greenspan "Alan Greenspan") to succeed Volcker, and Greenspan would lead the Federal Reserve until 2006. Greenspan raised interest rates in another attempt to curb inflation, setting off a [stock market crash](/wiki/Stock_market_crash "Stock market crash") in October 1987 known as "[Black Monday](/wiki/Black_Monday_(1987) "Black Monday (1987)")", but the markets stabilized and recovered in the following weeks.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015668–671-97)

### Labor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Labor")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/1907-_Income_inequality_and_union_participation.svg/330px-1907-_Income_inequality_and_union_participation.svg.png)](/wiki/File:1907-_Income_inequality_and_union_participation.svg)

Income inequality and union participation have had a distinctly inverse relationship, with the disparity increasing since the 1980s.[[98]](#cite_note-NYTimes_20230707-98)

In August 1981, the [Professional Air Traffic Controllers Organization](/wiki/Professional_Air_Traffic_Controllers_Organization_(1968) "Professional Air Traffic Controllers Organization (1968)") (PATCO), which consisted of federal employees, voted to go on a [labor strike](/wiki/Strike_action "Strike action") in hopes of receiving better pay and benefits. After the vote, Reagan announced that the strikers would be fired if they did not return to work within forty-eight hours. After the deadline passed, Reagan fired over 10,000 air traffic controllers, while approximately 40 percent of the union members returned to work. Reagan's handling of the strike was strongly criticized by union leaders, but it won the approval of his conservative base of voters and others in the public.[[99]](#cite_note-patterson157158-99)[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201586–87-100) The breaking of the PATCO strike demoralized organized labor, and the number of strikes fell dramatically in the 1980s.[[99]](#cite_note-patterson157158-99) Many of the strikes that did occur, including the [Arizona copper mine strike of 1983](/wiki/Arizona_copper_mine_strike_of_1983 "Arizona copper mine strike of 1983"), the 1983 [Greyhound](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines") bus driver strike, and the [1985–86 Hormel strike](/wiki/1985%E2%80%9386_Hormel_strike "1985–86 Hormel strike"), ended with dismissal of the strikers. With the assent of Reagan's sympathetic [National Labor Relations Board](/wiki/National_Labor_Relations_Board "National Labor Relations Board") appointees, many companies also won wage and benefit cutbacks from unions, especially in the manufacturing sector.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201587–88-101) During Reagan's time in office, the share of employees who were part of a labor union dropped from approximately one-fourth of the total workforce to approximately one-sixth of the total workforce.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005170-102)

### Deregulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Deregulation")]

Reagan sought to loosen federal regulation of economic activities, and he appointed key officials who shared this agenda. According to historian [William Leuchtenburg](/wiki/William_Leuchtenburg "William Leuchtenburg"), by 1986, the Reagan administration eliminated almost half of the federal regulations that had existed in 1981.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeuchtenburg2015601–604-103) The [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") aggressively deregulated the broadcasting industry, eliminating the [Fairness Doctrine](/wiki/Fairness_Doctrine "Fairness Doctrine") and other restrictions.[[104]](#cite_note-donnelly1-104) The 1982 [Garn–St. Germain Depository Institutions Act](/wiki/Garn%E2%80%93St._Germain_Depository_Institutions_Act "Garn–St. Germain Depository Institutions Act") deregulated [savings and loan associations](/wiki/Savings_and_loan_association "Savings and loan association") and allowed banks to provide [adjustable-rate mortgages](/wiki/Adjustable-rate_mortgage "Adjustable-rate mortgage"). Reagan also eliminated numerous government positions and dismissed numerous federal employees, including the entire staff of the [Employment and Training Administration](/wiki/Employment_and_Training_Administration "Employment and Training Administration"). Secretary of the Interior [James G. Watt](/wiki/James_G._Watt "James G. Watt") implemented policies designed to open up federal territories to oil drilling and [surface mining](/wiki/Surface_mining "Surface mining"). Under [EPA](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency") Director [Anne Gorsuch](/wiki/Anne_Gorsuch "Anne Gorsuch"), the EPA's budget was dramatically reduced and the EPA loosely enforced environmental regulations.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeuchtenburg2015601–604-103)

#### Savings and loan crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Savings and loan crisis")]

After the passage of the Garn–St. Germain Depository Institutions Act, savings and loans associations engaged in riskier activities, and the leaders of some institutions embezzled funds.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005175-105) In what became known as the [Savings and loan crisis](/wiki/Savings_and_loan_crisis "Savings and loan crisis"), a total of 747 financial institutions failed and needed to be rescued with $160 billion in taxpayer dollars.[[106]](#cite_note-106) As an indication of this scandal's size, [Martin Mayer](/wiki/Martin_Mayer "Martin Mayer") wrote at the time, "The theft from the taxpayer by the community that fattened on the growth of the savings and loan (S&L) industry in the 1980s is the worst public scandal in American history...Measuring by money, [or] by the misallocation of national resources...the S&L outrage makes [Teapot Dome](/wiki/Teapot_Dome_Scandal "Teapot Dome Scandal") and [Credit Mobilier](/wiki/Cr%C3%A9dit_Mobilier_of_America_scandal "Crédit Mobilier of America scandal") seem minor episodes."[[107]](#cite_note-107)

### Immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Immigration")]

See also: [Immigration to the United States](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States")

The 1980s saw the highest rate of immigration to the United States since the 1910s, and the proportion of the foreign-born population reached its highest level since the 1940s.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015158–159-108) Reagan did not make immigration a focus of his administration, but he came to support a package of reforms sponsored by Republican senator [Alan Simpson](/wiki/Alan_Simpson_(American_politician) "Alan Simpson (American politician)") and Democratic congressman [Romano Mazzoli](/wiki/Romano_Mazzoli "Romano Mazzoli"), which he signed into law as the [Immigration Reform and Control Act](/wiki/Immigration_Reform_and_Control_Act_of_1986 "Immigration Reform and Control Act of 1986") in November 1986.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015544–545-109) The act made it illegal to knowingly hire or recruit [illegal immigrants](/wiki/Illegal_immigration_to_the_United_States "Illegal immigration to the United States"), required employers to attest to their employees' immigration status, and granted amnesty to approximately three million illegal immigrants who had entered the United States before January 1, 1982, and had lived in the country continuously. The bill was also contained provisions designed to enhance security measures at the [Mexico–United States border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border").[[110]](#cite_note-bplumer1-110) Upon signing the act at a ceremony held beside the newly refurbished [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty"), Reagan said, "The legalization provisions in this act will go far to improve the lives of a class of individuals who now must hide in the shadows, without access to many of the benefits of a free and open society. Very soon many of these men and women will be able to step into the sunlight and, ultimately, if they choose, they may become Americans."[[111]](#cite_note-immigration-111) The bill was largely unsuccessful at halting illegal immigration, and the population of illegal immigrants rose from 5 million in 1986 to 11.1 million in 2013.[[110]](#cite_note-bplumer1-110)

### Criminal and anti-drug policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Criminal and anti-drug policy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Nancy_Reagan_at_a_%22Just_Say_No%22_rally_at_the_White_House.jpg/250px-Nancy_Reagan_at_a_%22Just_Say_No%22_rally_at_the_White_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nancy_Reagan_at_a_%22Just_Say_No%22_rally_at_the_White_House.jpg)

First Lady [Nancy Reagan](/wiki/Nancy_Reagan "Nancy Reagan") at a [Just Say No](/wiki/Just_Say_No "Just Say No") rally at the White House

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/US_incarceration_rate_timeline.gif/250px-US_incarceration_rate_timeline.gif)](/wiki/File:US_incarceration_rate_timeline.gif)

Graph demonstrating increases in [U.S. incarceration rate](/wiki/Incarceration_in_the_United_States "Incarceration in the United States")

Not long after being sworn into office, Reagan declared [more militant policies](/wiki/War_on_Drugs#Reagan "War on Drugs") in the "[war on drugs](/wiki/War_on_drugs "War on drugs")".[[112]](#cite_note-War_on_Drugs-112)[[113]](#cite_note-Youth_Trends-113) He promised a "planned, concerted campaign" against all drugs,[[114]](#cite_note-The_Drug_War-114) in hopes of decreasing drug use, particularly among adolescents.[[115]](#cite_note-Interview:_Dr._Herbert_Kleber-115)[[116]](#cite_note-Decline_of_Substance_Use-116) The "[crack epidemic](/wiki/Crack_epidemic "Crack epidemic")", which saw a large number of individuals become addicted to [crack cocaine](/wiki/Crack_cocaine "Crack cocaine") and may have played a role in numerous murders, emerged as a major area of public concern.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015122–124-117) [First Lady](/wiki/First_Lady_of_the_United_States "First Lady of the United States") Nancy Reagan made the war on drugs her main cause as First Lady, founding the "[Just Say No](/wiki/Just_Say_No "Just Say No")" drug awareness campaign.[[118]](#cite_note-PBS_Frontline-118)

Concerns about drug use prompted Congress to pass legislation such as the [Comprehensive Crime Control Act of 1984](/wiki/Comprehensive_Crime_Control_Act_of_1984 "Comprehensive Crime Control Act of 1984")[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015124-119) and the [Anti-Drug Abuse Act of 1986](/wiki/Anti-Drug_Abuse_Act_of_1986 "Anti-Drug Abuse Act of 1986"), the latter of which granted $1.7 billion to fight drugs and established a mandatory minimum penalties for drug offenses.[[118]](#cite_note-PBS_Frontline-118) Reagan also signed the [Anti-Drug Abuse Act of 1988](/wiki/Anti-Drug_Abuse_Act_of_1988 "Anti-Drug Abuse Act of 1988"), which further increased criminal penalties for drug use and established the [Office of National Drug Control Policy](/wiki/Office_of_National_Drug_Control_Policy "Office of National Drug Control Policy").[[120]](#cite_note-jjohnson1-120) Critics charged that Reagan's policies promoted significant racial disparities in the prison population,[[118]](#cite_note-PBS_Frontline-118) were ineffective in reducing the availability of drugs or crime on the street, and came at a great financial and human cost for American society.[[121]](#cite_note-Stop_the_Drug_War-121) Supporters argued that the numbers for adolescent drug users declined during Reagan's years in office.[[116]](#cite_note-Decline_of_Substance_Use-116)

On May 19, 1986, President Reagan signed the [Firearm Owners Protection Act](/wiki/Firearm_Owners_Protection_Act "Firearm Owners Protection Act"), which amended the [Gun Control Act of 1968](/wiki/Gun_Control_Act_of_1968 "Gun Control Act of 1968"), prohibiting the transfer or possession of [machine guns](/wiki/Machine_guns "Machine guns").[[122]](#cite_note-122) In 1989, Reagan said "I do not believe in taking away the right of the citizen to own guns for sporting, for hunting and so forth, or for home defense; but I do believe that an [AK-47](/wiki/AK-47 "AK-47"), a machine gun, is not a sporting weapon or needed for defense of a home."[[123]](#cite_note-123)[[124]](#cite_note-124)

### Social policies and civil rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Social policies and civil rights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Blood_on_its_hands.png/250px-Blood_on_its_hands.png)](/wiki/File:Blood_on_its_hands.png)

The Reagan administration was criticized both contemporaneously, as seen in this 1988 poster from [ACT-UP](/wiki/ACT_UP "ACT UP"), and retrospectively for its [response to the AIDS epidemic](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_and_AIDS "Ronald Reagan and AIDS").

Reagan was largely unable to enact his ambitious social policy agenda, which included a federal ban on [abortions](/wiki/Abortions_in_the_United_States "Abortions in the United States") and an end to [desegregation busing](/wiki/Desegregation_busing "Desegregation busing").[[125]](#cite_note-svroberts1-125) Despite the lack of major social policy legislation, Reagan was able to influence social policy through regulations and the appointment of conservative Supreme Court Justices.[[125]](#cite_note-svroberts1-125)

#### School prayer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: School prayer")]

With Reagan's support, conservative Republican senator [Jesse Helms](/wiki/Jesse_Helms "Jesse Helms") led an effort to prevent the Supreme Court from reviewing state and local laws mandating [school prayer](/wiki/School_prayer "School prayer"), but Republican senators like [Lowell Weicker](/wiki/Lowell_Weicker "Lowell Weicker") and [Barry Goldwater](/wiki/Barry_Goldwater "Barry Goldwater") blocked passage of Helms' bill.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201595–96-126) Reagan campaigned vigorously to restore organized prayer to the schools, first as a moment of prayer and later as a moment of silence.[[127]](#cite_note-127) His election reflected an opposition[[128]](#cite_note-Law_of_Church_and_State-128) to *[Engel v. Vitale](/wiki/Engel_v._Vitale "Engel v. Vitale")*, which prohibited state officials from composing an official state prayer and requiring that it be recited in the public schools.[[129]](#cite_note-129) In 1981, he proposed a constitutional amendment [on school prayer](/wiki/School_prayer_in_the_United_States "School prayer in the United States"),[[128]](#cite_note-Law_of_Church_and_State-128) which stated: "Nothing in this Constitution shall be construed to prohibit individual or group prayer in public schools or other public institutions. No person shall be required by the United States or by any state to participate in prayer." In 1984, he again raised the issue to Congress.[[130]](#cite_note-130) In 1985, he expressed his disappointment that the Supreme Court ruling still banned a moment of silence for public schools, and said that efforts to reinstitute prayer in public schools were "an uphill battle".[[131]](#cite_note-131) In 1987, he renewed his call for Congress to support voluntary prayer in schools.[[132]](#cite_note-NYT-132)

#### Civil rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Civil rights")]

In 1982, Reagan signed a bill extending the [Voting Rights Act](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act "Voting Rights Act") for 25 years after a grass-roots lobbying and legislative campaign forced him to abandon his plan to ease that law's restrictions.[[133]](#cite_note-133) He also reluctantly accepted the continuation of [affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action_in_the_United_States "Affirmative action in the United States") programs[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005171-134) and the establishment of [Martin Luther King Jr. Day](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr._Day "Martin Luther King Jr. Day") as a [federal holiday](/wiki/Federal_holidays_in_the_United_States "Federal holidays in the United States").[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015163–164-135) The [Equal Employment Opportunity Commission](/wiki/Equal_Employment_Opportunity_Commission "Equal Employment Opportunity Commission") and the Justice Department both prosecuted far fewer civil rights cases per year than they had under Carter.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201542–43-136) In 1988, Reagan vetoed the [Civil Rights Restoration Act](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Restoration_Act "Civil Rights Restoration Act"), but his veto was overridden by Congress. Reagan had argued that the legislation infringed on [states' rights](/wiki/States%27_rights "States' rights") and the rights of churches and business owners.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

#### LGBTQ rights and AIDS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: LGBTQ rights and AIDS")]

Main article: [Ronald Reagan and AIDS](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_and_AIDS "Ronald Reagan and AIDS")

No civil rights legislation for [gay](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality") individuals passed during Reagan's tenure. Many in the Reagan administration, including [Communications Director](/wiki/White_House_Communications_Director "White House Communications Director") [Pat Buchanan](/wiki/Pat_Buchanan "Pat Buchanan"), were hostile to the gay community, as were many religious leaders who were important allies to the administration.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilentz2008185–186-138) Gay rights and the growing [HIV/AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS") emerged as an important matter of public concern in 1985 after it was disclosed that actor [Rock Hudson](/wiki/Rock_Hudson "Rock Hudson"), a personal friend of President Reagan, was receiving treatment for AIDS. As public anxiety over AIDS rose, the Supreme Court upheld a state law that criminalized homosexuality in the case of *[Bowers v. Hardwick](/wiki/Bowers_v._Hardwick "Bowers v. Hardwick")*.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015132–134-139) Though Surgeon General [C. Everett Koop](/wiki/C._Everett_Koop "C. Everett Koop") advocated for a public health campaign designed to reduce the spread of AIDS by raising awareness and promoting the use of condoms, Reagan rejected Koop's proposals in favor of [abstinence-only sex education](/wiki/Abstinence-only_sex_education "Abstinence-only sex education").[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015212–214-140) By 1989, approximately 60,000 Americans had died of AIDS, and liberals strongly criticized Reagan's response to the HIV/AIDS crisis.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005179–182-141) On the 1980 campaign trail, Reagan spoke of the gay rights movement:

> My criticism is that the gay movement isn't just asking for civil rights; it's asking for recognition and acceptance of an alternative lifestyle which I do not believe society can condone, nor can I.[[142]](#cite_note-142)

### Environmental policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Environmental policy")]

Reagan's strong preferences for limited federal involvement and deregulation extended to the environment. His main goal was to lessen the burden of regulation on businesses to promote more economic activity in the United States. Because of this policy, Reagan refused to renew the Clean Air Act during his administration.[[143]](#cite_note-143) Reagan lessened existing regulations on pollution, cut funding to government environmental agencies, and appointed known anti-environmentalist individuals to key positions presiding over these organizations.[[144]](#cite_note-144)

When Reagan took office in 1981, he "attempted to reduce" money that was directed towards studying the burgeoning field of global warming and human-driven climate change.[[145]](#cite_note-145) In the early 1980s, the study of the intersection between human activity and climate change was still in its infancy and scientists were far from a consensus on the topic.[[146]](#cite_note-146)

In 1987, the Reagan administration signed the [Montreal Protocol](/wiki/Montreal_Protocol "Montreal Protocol") in an effort to reduce emissions that damage the [ozone layer](/wiki/Ozone_layer "Ozone layer").[[147]](#cite_note-147)

### Mass surveillance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Mass surveillance")]

Citing [national security](/wiki/National_security "National security") concerns, the president's national security team pressed for more [surveillance](/wiki/Surveillance "Surveillance") power early during Reagan's first term. Their recommendations were based upon the [premise](/wiki/Premise "Premise") that the federal government's [intelligence](/wiki/Intelligence_assessment "Intelligence assessment") and [counterintelligence](/wiki/Counterintelligence "Counterintelligence") capabilities had been weakened by presidents Carter and Ford.[[148]](#cite_note-148) On December 4, 1981, Reagan signed [Executive Order 12333](/wiki/Executive_Order_12333 "Executive Order 12333"). This [presidential directive](/wiki/Presidential_directive "Presidential directive") broadened the power of the [government's intelligence community](/wiki/United_States_Intelligence_Community "United States Intelligence Community"); mandated rules for spying on United States citizens, permanent residents, and on anyone within the United States; and also directed the attorney general and others to create further policies and procedures for what information intelligence agencies can collect, retain, and share.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

## Foreign affairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Foreign affairs")]

Main article: [Foreign policy of the Reagan administration](/wiki/Foreign_policy_of_the_Reagan_administration "Foreign policy of the Reagan administration")

### Escalation of the Cold War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Escalation of the Cold War")]

See also: [Cold War (1979–1985)](/wiki/Cold_War_(1979%E2%80%931985) "Cold War (1979–1985)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Ronald_Reagan_Overseas_Visits.svg/500px-Ronald_Reagan_Overseas_Visits.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ronald_Reagan_Overseas_Visits.svg)

[Reagan made 24 international trips](/wiki/List_of_international_presidential_trips_made_by_Ronald_Reagan "List of international presidential trips made by Ronald Reagan") to 26 different countries during his presidency.[[150]](#cite_note-150)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/President_Ronald_Reagan_addressing_British_Parliament_in_London%2C_England.jpg/250px-President_Ronald_Reagan_addressing_British_Parliament_in_London%2C_England.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Ronald_Reagan_addressing_British_Parliament_in_London,_England.jpg)

As the first U.S. president invited to speak before the [British Parliament](/wiki/British_Parliament "British Parliament") (June 8, 1982), Reagan predicted [Marxism-Leninism](/wiki/Marxism-Leninism "Marxism-Leninism") would end up on the "[ash heap of history](/wiki/Ash_heap_of_history "Ash heap of history")"[[151]](#cite_note-151)

Reagan escalated the Cold War, accelerating a reversal from the policy of détente which had begun in 1979 after the [Soviet invasion](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War") of [Afghanistan](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_Afghanistan "Democratic Republic of Afghanistan").[[152]](#cite_note-152) Reagan feared that the Soviet Union had gained a military advantage over the United States, and the Reagan administration hoped that heightened military spending would grant the U.S. military superiority and weaken the Soviet economy.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201566–67-153) Reagan ordered a massive buildup of the [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces"), directing funding to the [B-1 Lancer](/wiki/B-1_Lancer "B-1 Lancer") bomber, the [B-2 Spirit bomber](/wiki/Northrop_Grumman_B-2_Spirit "Northrop Grumman B-2 Spirit"), [cruise missiles](/wiki/Cruise_missile "Cruise missile"), the [MX missile](/wiki/LGM-118_Peacekeeper "LGM-118 Peacekeeper"), and the [600-ship Navy](/wiki/600-ship_Navy "600-ship Navy").[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005200-154) In response to Soviet deployment of the [SS-20](/wiki/SS-20 "SS-20"), Reagan oversaw [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO")'s deployment of the [Pershing missile](/wiki/Pershing_missile "Pershing missile") in West Germany.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005205-155) The president also strongly denounced the Soviet Union and Communism in moral terms,[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201567-156) describing the Soviet Union as an "[evil empire](/wiki/Evil_Empire_speech "Evil Empire speech")".[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECannon2000314–317-157) Despite this heavy rhetoric,[[158]](#cite_note-158) the Reagan administration continued arms control talks with the Soviet Union in the form of "[START](/wiki/START_I "START I")". Unlike the "[SALT](/wiki/Strategic_Arms_Limitation_Talks "Strategic Arms Limitation Talks")" treaties of the 1970s, which set upper limits on the size of nuclear arsenals, the proposed START treaty would require both sides to reduce their existing nuclear arsenals.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008868–869-159)

#### Strategic Defense Initiative

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Strategic Defense Initiative")]

In March 1983, Reagan introduced the [Strategic Defense Initiative](/wiki/Strategic_Defense_Initiative "Strategic Defense Initiative") (SDI), a defense project that would have used ground- and space-based systems to protect the United States from attack by strategic nuclear ballistic missiles. Reagan believed that this defense shield could make [nuclear war](/wiki/Nuclear_war "Nuclear war") impossible.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeschloss2007293-160) Many scientists and national security experts criticized the project as costly and technologically infeasible, and critics dubbed SDI as "Star Wars" in reference to a [popular film series of the same name](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars").[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008870–871-161) Ultimately, the SDI would be canceled in 1993 due to concerns about its cost and effectiveness as well as a changing international situation.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015725–726-162) However, the Soviets became concerned about the possible effects SDI would have and viewed its development as a violation of the [Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty](/wiki/Anti-Ballistic_Missile_Treaty "Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty").[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015581–585-163) In protest of SDI, the Soviet Union broke off arms control talks, and U.S.-Soviet relations descended to their lowest point since the early 1960s.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008869–870-164) The Cold War tensions influenced works of popular culture such as the films *[The Day After](/wiki/The_Day_After "The Day After")* and *[WarGames](/wiki/WarGames "WarGames")* (both 1983), and the song "[99 Luftballons](/wiki/99_Luftballons "99 Luftballons")" (1983) by [Nena](/wiki/Nena_(band) "Nena (band)"), each of which exhibited the rising public anxiety for the possibility of a nuclear war.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015116–117-165)

### Reagan Doctrine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Reagan Doctrine")]

Main article: [Reagan Doctrine](/wiki/Reagan_Doctrine "Reagan Doctrine")

See also: [Soviet–Afghan War](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Reagan_sitting_with_people_from_the_Afghanistan-Pakistan_region_in_February_1983.jpg/250px-Reagan_sitting_with_people_from_the_Afghanistan-Pakistan_region_in_February_1983.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reagan_sitting_with_people_from_the_Afghanistan-Pakistan_region_in_February_1983.jpg)

Meeting with leaders of the Afghan [Mujahideen](/wiki/Mujahideen "Mujahideen") in the Oval Office, 1983

Under a policy that came to be known as the [Reagan Doctrine](/wiki/Reagan_Doctrine "Reagan Doctrine"), the Reagan administration provided overt and covert aid to anti-communist [resistance movements](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") in an effort to "[rollback](/wiki/Rollback "Rollback")" Soviet-backed communist governments in Africa, Asia, and Latin America.[[166]](#cite_note-166) In Eastern Europe, the CIA provided support to the Polish opposition group, [Solidarity](/wiki/Solidarity_(Polish_trade_union) "Solidarity (Polish trade union)"), ensuring that it stayed afloat during a [period of martial law](/wiki/Martial_law_in_Poland "Martial law in Poland").[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008883–884-167) Reagan deployed the CIA's [Special Activities Division](/wiki/Special_Activities_Division "Special Activities Division") to Afghanistan and Pakistan, and the CIA was instrumental in training, equipping, and leading [Mujahideen](/wiki/Islamic_Unity_of_Afghanistan_Mujahideen "Islamic Unity of Afghanistan Mujahideen") forces against the Soviet Army in the [Soviet–Afghan War](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War").[[168]](#cite_note-Crile_2003-168)[[169]](#cite_note-169) By 1987, the United States was sending over $600 million a year, as well as weapons, intelligence, and combat expertise to Afghanistan. The Soviet Union announced it would withdraw from Afghanistan in 1987, but the U.S. was subjected to [blowback](/wiki/Blowback_(intelligence) "Blowback (intelligence)") in the form of the [Taliban](/wiki/Taliban "Taliban") and [al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda"), two groups that arose out of the Mujahideen and that would oppose the United States in future conflicts.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008883–884-167)

Although leading conservatives argued that Reagan's foreign policy strategy was essential to protecting their security interests, critics labeled the initiatives as aggressive and imperialistic, and chided them as "warmongering".[[170]](#cite_note-PBS-170) Reagan was also heavily criticized for backing [anti-communist](/wiki/Anti-communist "Anti-communist") leaders accused of severe [human rights violations](/wiki/Human_rights_violations "Human rights violations"), such as [Hissène Habré](/wiki/Hiss%C3%A8ne_Habr%C3%A9 "Hissène Habré")[[171]](#cite_note-171) and [Efraín Ríos Montt](/wiki/Efra%C3%ADn_R%C3%ADos_Montt "Efraín Ríos Montt").[[172]](#cite_note-172)[[173]](#cite_note-173) Montt was the [president of Guatemala](/wiki/President_of_Guatemala "President of Guatemala") and the [Guatemalan military](/wiki/Guatemalan_military "Guatemalan military") was accused [of genocide](/wiki/Guatemalan_genocide "Guatemalan genocide") for massacres of members of the [Ixil people](/wiki/Ixil_people "Ixil people") and other indigenous groups.[[174]](#cite_note-174) Reagan had said that Montt was getting a "bum rap",[[175]](#cite_note-ABC_News_on_Guatemala,_May_14,_2013-175) and described him as "a man of great personal integrity".[[176]](#cite_note-176) Previous human rights violations had prompted the United States to cut off aid to the Guatemalan government, but the Reagan administration unsuccessfully appealed to Congress to restart military aid. However, the administration successfully provided nonmilitary assistance such as the [United States Agency for International Development](/wiki/United_States_Agency_for_International_Development "United States Agency for International Development").[[175]](#cite_note-ABC_News_on_Guatemala,_May_14,_2013-175)[[177]](#cite_note-177)

### Central America and the Caribbean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Central America and the Caribbean")]

See also: [Latin America–United States relations](/wiki/Latin_America%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Latin America–United States relations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/President_Ronald_Reagan_meets_with_Prime_Minister_Eugenia_Charles.jpg/250px-President_Ronald_Reagan_meets_with_Prime_Minister_Eugenia_Charles.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Ronald_Reagan_meets_with_Prime_Minister_Eugenia_Charles.jpg)

Reagan meets with Prime Minister [Eugenia Charles](/wiki/Eugenia_Charles "Eugenia Charles") of [Dominica](/wiki/Dominica "Dominica") in the [Oval Office](/wiki/Oval_Office "Oval Office") about ongoing events in [Grenada](/wiki/Grenada "Grenada")

The Reagan administration placed a high priority on the Central America and the [Caribbean Sea](/wiki/Caribbean_Sea "Caribbean Sea"), which it saw as a key front in the Cold War. Reagan and his foreign policy team were particularly concerned about the potential influence of [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") on countries such as [Grenada](/wiki/Grenada "Grenada"), [Nicaragua](/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua"), and [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"). To counter the influence of Cuba and the Soviet Union, Reagan launched the [Caribbean Basin Initiative](/wiki/Caribbean_Basin_Initiative "Caribbean Basin Initiative"), an economic program designed to aid countries opposed to Communism.
