He also authorized covert measures, such as the arming of Nicaragua's [Contras](/wiki/Contras "Contras"), to minimize Cuban and Soviet influence in the region.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015350–357-178) The administration provided support to right-wing governments throughout Latin America, disregarding humans rights abuses in countries like [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") and El Salvador.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow201573,_77–79-179)

#### Invasion of Grenada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Invasion of Grenada")]

Tensions rose between the left-wing [Grenadan](/wiki/Grenada "Grenada") government of [Maurice Bishop](/wiki/Maurice_Bishop "Maurice Bishop") and the U.S. because Cuban construction workers were building an airfield on the island. On October 16, 1983, pro-Communist forces of [Hudson Austin](/wiki/Hudson_Austin "Hudson Austin") led a coup against Bishop, who was subsequently arrested and executed. Reagan dispatched approximately 5,000 U.S. soldiers to [invade Grenada](/wiki/United_States_invasion_of_Grenada "United States invasion of Grenada") nine days after. After two days of fighting that resulted in the deaths of 19 Americans, 45 Grenadans, and 24 Cubans, Austin's government was overthrown.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005205–206-180) Reagan then declared, "Our days of weakness are over. Our military forces are back on their feet and standing tall."[[181]](#cite_note-181) While the invasion enjoyed public support in the United States and Grenada,[[182]](#cite_note-Magnuson-182)[[183]](#cite_note-183) it was criticized by the United Kingdom, Canada and the [United Nations General Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly") as "a flagrant violation of [international law](/wiki/International_law "International law")".[[184]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-184)

### Iran–Contra affair

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Iran–Contra affair")]

Main article: [Iran–Contra affair](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Contra_affair "Iran–Contra affair")

See also: [Scandals of the Reagan administration](/wiki/Scandals_of_the_Reagan_administration "Scandals of the Reagan administration")

[![Reagan in the Cabinet Room to receive the Tower Commission Report on the Iran–Contra affair, February 1987](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Photograph_of_President_Reagan_receiving_the_Tower_Commission_Report_in_the_Cabinet_Room_-_NARA_-_198581.jpg/250px-Photograph_of_President_Reagan_receiving_the_Tower_Commission_Report_in_the_Cabinet_Room_-_NARA_-_198581.jpg)](/wiki/File:Photograph_of_President_Reagan_receiving_the_Tower_Commission_Report_in_the_Cabinet_Room_-_NARA_-_198581.jpg)

Reagan receiving the [Tower Commission](/wiki/Tower_Commission "Tower Commission") on the [Iran–Contra affair](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Contra_affair "Iran–Contra affair"), 1987

In 1979, a group of left-wing rebels in Nicaragua known as the [Sandinistas](/wiki/Sandinistas "Sandinistas") overthrew the president of Nicaragua and installed [Daniel Ortega](/wiki/Daniel_Ortega "Daniel Ortega") as the country's leader.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005207–208-185) Fearing that Communists would take over Nicaragua if it remained under the leadership of the Sandinistas, the Reagan administration authorized CIA Director William J. Casey to arm the right-wing [Contras](/wiki/Contras "Contras"). Congress, which favored negotiations between the Contras and Sandinista, passed the 1982 [Boland Amendment](/wiki/Boland_Amendment "Boland Amendment"), prohibiting the CIA and Defense Department from using their budgets to provide aid to the Contras. Still intent on supporting the Contras, the Reagan administration raised funds for the Contras from private donors and foreign governments.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeisberg2016128–129-186) When Congress learned that the CIA had secretly placed [naval mines](/wiki/Naval_mine "Naval mine") in Nicaraguan harbors, Congress passed a second Boland Amendment that barred granting any assistance to the Contras.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005208–209-187)

During his second term, Reagan sought to find a way procure the release of [seven American hostages](/wiki/Lebanon_hostage_crisis "Lebanon hostage crisis") held by [Hezbollah](/wiki/Hezbollah "Hezbollah"), a Lebanese paramilitary group supported by Iran. The Reagan administration decided to sell American arms to Iran, then engaged in the [Iran–Iraq War](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Iraq_War "Iran–Iraq War"), in hopes that Iran would pressure Hezbollah to release the hostages.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeisberg2016129–134-188) Secretary of Defense Weinberger and Secretary of State Shultz both opposed the arrangement, so it was handled by National Security Advisor [Robert McFarlane](/wiki/Robert_McFarlane_(American_politician) "Robert McFarlane (American politician)") and McFarlane's successor, [John Poindexter](/wiki/John_Poindexter "John Poindexter").[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005209–210-189) The Reagan administration sold over 2000 missiles to Iran without informing Congress; Hezbollah released four hostages but captured an additional six Americans. On the initiative of [Oliver North](/wiki/Oliver_North "Oliver North"), an aide on the National Security Council, the Reagan administration redirected the proceeds from the missile sales to the Contras.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeisberg2016129–134-188) The transactions became public knowledge by early November 1986. Reagan initially denied any wrongdoing, but on November 25 he announced that Poindexter and North had left the administration and that he would form the [Tower Commission](/wiki/Tower_Commission "Tower Commission") to investigate the transactions. A few weeks later, Reagan asked a panel of federal judges to appoint a [special prosecutor](/wiki/United_States_Office_of_the_Independent_Counsel "United States Office of the Independent Counsel") who would conduct a separate investigation, and the panel chose [Lawrence Walsh](/wiki/Lawrence_Walsh "Lawrence Walsh").[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005210–211-190)

The Tower Commission, chaired by former Republican senator [John Tower](/wiki/John_Tower "John Tower"), released a report in February 1987 that confirmed that the administration had traded arms for hostages and sent the proceeds of the weapons sales to the Contras. The report laid most of the blame for the operation on North, Poindexter, and McFarlane, but it was also critical of Regan and other White House staffers.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015646–649-191) In response to the Tower Commission report, Reagan stated, "Its findings are honest, convincing and highly critical...As angry as I may be about activities undertaken without my knowledge, I am still accountable for those activities."[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015650–653-192) The [Iran–Contra scandal](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Contra_scandal "Iran–Contra scandal"), as it became known, did serious damage to the Reagan presidency, raising questions about Reagan's competency and the wisdom of conservative policies.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015202–204-193) A poll taken in March 1987 showed that 85 percent of respondents believed that the Reagan administration had engaged in an organized cover-up, and half of the respondents believed that Reagan had been personally involved. The administration's credibility was also badly damaged on the international stage, as it had violated its own arms embargo on Iran.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015653,_674-194) Congressional Democrats considered [impeaching](/wiki/Federal_impeachment_in_the_United_States "Federal impeachment in the United States"), but decided that it would be an unwise use of political capital against a weakened president; Democrats were also somewhat mollified by Reagan's decision to replace Chief of Staff Regan with Howard Baker.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015216–217-195)

The investigations into the Iran–Contra scandal continued after Reagan left office, but were effectively halted when President George H. W. Bush pardoned Secretary of Defense Caspar Weinberger before his trial began.[[196]](#cite_note-196) Investigators did not find conclusive proof that Reagan had known about the aid provided to the Contras, but Walsh's report noted that Reagan had "created the conditions which made possible the crimes committed by others" and had "knowingly participated or acquiesced in covering up the scandal".[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005211–212-197)

### End of the Cold War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: End of the Cold War")]

See also: [Cold War (1985–1991)](/wiki/Cold_War_(1985%E2%80%931991) "Cold War (1985–1991)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Reagan_and_Gorbachev_signing.jpg/250px-Reagan_and_Gorbachev_signing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reagan_and_Gorbachev_signing.jpg)

Gorbachev and Reagan sign the [INF Treaty](/wiki/INF_Treaty "INF Treaty") at the White House, 1987

Three different [Soviet leaders](/wiki/List_of_leaders_of_the_Soviet_Union "List of leaders of the Soviet Union") died between 1982 and 1985, leaving the Soviets with an unstable leadership until [Mikhail Gorbachev](/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev "Mikhail Gorbachev") came to power in 1985.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008894-198) Although the Soviet Union had not accelerated military spending during Reagan's military buildup,[[199]](#cite_note-199) their large military expenses, in combination with [collectivized agriculture](/wiki/Collectivization_in_the_Soviet_Union "Collectivization in the Soviet Union") and inefficient [planned manufacturing](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy"), were a heavy burden for the [Soviet economy](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Soviet_Union "Economy of the Soviet Union").[[200]](#cite_note-Gaidar-200) Gorbachev was less ideologically rigid than his predecessors, and he believed that the Soviet Union urgently needed economic and political reforms.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008894-198) In 1986, he introduced his twin reforms of [perestroika](/wiki/Perestroika "Perestroika") and [glasnost](/wiki/Glasnost "Glasnost"), which would change the political and economic conditions of the Soviet Union.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015675–676-201) Seeking to reduce military expenditures and minimize the possibility of nuclear war, he also sought to re-open negotiations with the United States over arms control.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008894-198)

As his influence on domestic affairs waned during his second term, Reagan increasingly focused on relations with the Soviet Union.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015225–226-202) Reagan recognized the change in the direction of the Soviet leadership under Gorbachev, and shifted to diplomacy, with a view to encourage the Soviet leader to pursue substantial arms agreements. Reagan's personal mission was to achieve a world free of nuclear weapons, which according to [Jack F. Matlock Jr.](/wiki/Jack_F._Matlock_Jr. "Jack F. Matlock Jr."), Reagan's ambassador to Moscow, he regarded as "totally irrational, totally inhumane, good for nothing but killing, possibly destructive of life on earth and civilization".[[203]](#cite_note-203) Gorbachev and Reagan agreed to meet at the 1985 [Geneva Summit](/wiki/Geneva_Summit_(1985) "Geneva Summit (1985)"), where they issued a joint statement indicating that neither the U.S. nor the Soviet Union would "seek to achieve military superiority".[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015225–227-204) The two leaders began a private correspondence after the summit, and each became increasingly optimistic about arms control negotiations.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008895–896-205) Reagan's willingness to negotiate with the Soviets was opposed by many conservatives, including Weinberger; conservative columnist [George Will](/wiki/George_Will "George Will") wrote that Reagan was "elevating wishful thinking to the status of a political philosophy".[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005214–215-206)

Various issues, including intelligence operations performed by both countries and tensions in Germany and Afghanistan, threatened to forestall the possibility of an agreement between the United States and the Soviet Union. Nonetheless, both Gorbachev and Reagan agreed to continue arms control negotiations at the October 1986 [Reykjavík Summit](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk_Summit "Reykjavík Summit").[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015228–230-207) At the summit, Gorbachev and Reagan closed in on an agreement to greatly reduce or eliminate the nuclear stockpiles of both the U.S. and the Soviet Union over a ten-year period, but the deal collapsed due to disagreements regarding SDI development.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015596–604-208) Reagan attacked Gorbachev in a [1987 speech](/wiki/Tear_down_this_wall! "Tear down this wall!") delivered in [West Berlin](/wiki/West_Berlin "West Berlin"), but negotiations continued.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005215-209) Gorbachev and Reagan broke the impasse by agreeing to negotiate separate treaties on intermediate nuclear forces (such as [intermediate-range ballistic missiles](/wiki/Intermediate-range_ballistic_missile "Intermediate-range ballistic missile")) and strategic arms (such as [intercontinental ballistic missiles](/wiki/Intercontinental_ballistic_missile "Intercontinental ballistic missile")).[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015676–677-210)

With the framework for an agreement in place, Reagan and Gorbachev met at the 1987 [Washington Summit](/wiki/Washington_Summit_(1987) "Washington Summit (1987)").[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015234–235-211) They signed the [Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty](/wiki/Intermediate-Range_Nuclear_Forces_Treaty "Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty") (INF Treaty), which committed both signatories to the total abolition of their respective short-range and medium-range missile stockpiles.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005215-209) The agreement marked the first time that the United States and the Soviet Union had committed to the elimination of a type of nuclear weapon, though it provided for the dismantlement of only about one-twentieth of the worldwide nuclear weapon arsenal. The treaty also established an inspections regime designed to ensure that both parties honored the agreement.[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015236-212) In addition to the INF Treaty, Reagan and Gorbachev discussed a potential strategic arms treaty, known as START, but SDI continued to be a major point of contention.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015682–685-213) In May 1988, the Senate voted 93-to-5 in favor of ratifying the INF Treaty.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005216-214)

President Reagan's Trip to USSR, Walking in Red Square with Mikhail Gorbachev, Moscow, May 31, 1988

Though it was attacked by conservatives like Jesse Helms, the INF Treaty provided a major boost to Reagan's popularity in the aftermath of the Iran–Contra Affair. A new era of trade and openness between the two powers commenced, and the U.S. and the Soviet Union cooperated on international issues such as the [Iran–Iraq War](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93Iraq_War "Iran–Iraq War").[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008897–898-215) When Reagan visited Moscow for a [fourth summit](/wiki/Moscow_Summit_(1988) "Moscow Summit (1988)") with Gorbachev in 1988, he was viewed as a celebrity by the Soviets. A journalist asked the president if he still considered the Soviet Union the evil empire. "No," he replied, "I was talking about another time, another era."[[216]](#cite_note-216) At Gorbachev's request, Reagan gave a speech on free markets at the [Moscow State University](/wiki/Moscow_State_University "Moscow State University").[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReagan1990713-217) In December 1988, Gorbachev effectively renounced the [Brezhnev Doctrine](/wiki/Brezhnev_Doctrine "Brezhnev Doctrine"), paving the way for democratization in Eastern Europe.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008898–899-218) In November 1989, ten months after Reagan left office, the [Berlin Wall](/wiki/Berlin_Wall "Berlin Wall") [fell](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Berlin_Wall "Fall of the Berlin Wall"). The Cold War was unofficially declared over at the [Malta Summit](/wiki/Malta_Summit "Malta Summit") the following month.[[219]](#cite_note-219)

### Honoring German war dead at Bitburg, Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Honoring German war dead at Bitburg, Germany")]

Main article: [Bitburg controversy](/wiki/Bitburg_controversy "Bitburg controversy")

Reagan came under much criticism in 1985 when he was accused of honoring Nazi war criminals at a cemetery in West Germany.[[220]](#cite_note-220) In February 1985, the administration accepted an invitation for Reagan to visit a German military cemetery in [Bitburg](/wiki/Bitburg "Bitburg") and to place a wreath alongside West German chancellor [Helmut Kohl](/wiki/Helmut_Kohl "Helmut Kohl"). Deaver was given assurances by a German head of protocol that no war criminals were buried there. It was later determined that the cemetery held the graves of 49 members of the [Waffen-SS](/wiki/Waffen-SS "Waffen-SS"). What neither Deaver nor other administration officials initially realized was that many Germans distinguished the regular SS, who typically were composed of Nazi true believers, and the Waffen-SS which were attached to military units and composed of conscripted soldiers.[[221]](#cite_note-221)

As the controversy brewed in April 1985, Reagan issued a statement that called the Nazi soldiers buried in that cemetery as themselves "victims", a designation which ignited a stir over whether Reagan had equated the SS men to victims of [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust").[[222]](#cite_note-222) [Pat Buchanan](/wiki/Pat_Buchanan "Pat Buchanan"), Reagan's director of communications, argued that the president did not equate the SS members with the actual Holocaust, but as victims of the ideology of Nazism.[[223]](#cite_note-223) Now strongly urged to cancel the visit,[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves2005249-224) the president responded that it would be wrong to back down on a promise he had made to Chancellor Kohl. On May 5, 1985, President Reagan and Chancellor Kohl first visited the site of the former Nazi Bergen-Belsen concentration camp and then the Bitburg cemetery where, along with two military generals, they did place a wreath.[[225]](#cite_note-225)[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReeves2005255-226)

### Middle East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Middle East")]

[![Reagan and Sadat 1981](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Reagan_and_Sadat_1981.jpg/250px-Reagan_and_Sadat_1981.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reagan_and_Sadat_1981.jpg)

Egyptian president [Anwar Sadat](/wiki/Anwar_Sadat "Anwar Sadat") with Reagan in a joint press conference at the White House, 1981

#### Lebanon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Lebanon")]

A [civil war](/wiki/Lebanese_Civil_War "Lebanese Civil War") had broken out in [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") in 1975, and both [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") undertook military action within Lebanon in 1982.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015366–367-227) After Israel [invaded](/wiki/1982_Lebanon_War "1982 Lebanon War") [Southern Lebanon](/wiki/Southern_Lebanon "Southern Lebanon"), Reagan faced domestic and international pressure to oppose the Israeli invasion, but Reagan was reluctant to openly break Israel. Reagan sympathized with Israeli's desire to defeat [PLO](/wiki/Palestine_Liberation_Organization "Palestine Liberation Organization") forces that had struck Israel from Lebanon, but he pressured Israel to end its invasion as casualties mounted and Israeli forces approached the Lebanese capital of [Beirut](/wiki/Beirut "Beirut").[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015382–385-228) American diplomat [Philip Habib](/wiki/Philip_Habib "Philip Habib") arranged a cease-fire in which Israel, Syria, and the PLO, all agreed to evacuate their forces from Lebanon. As Israel delayed a full withdrawal and violence continued in Lebanon, Reagan arranged for a [multinational force](/wiki/Multinational_Force_in_Lebanon "Multinational Force in Lebanon"), including [U.S. Marines](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps"), to serve as peacekeepers in Lebanon.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015386–389-229) In October 1983, [two nearly-simultaneous bombings](/wiki/1983_Beirut_barracks_bombings "1983 Beirut barracks bombings") in Beirut killed 241 American Marines and 58 French soldiers.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015394–395-230) The international peacekeeping force was withdrawn from Lebanon in 1984. In reaction to the role Israel and the United States played in the Lebanese Civil War, a [Shia](/wiki/Shia_Islam "Shia Islam") militant group known as [Hezbollah](/wiki/Hezbollah "Hezbollah") began to take American hostages, holding eight Americans by the middle of 1985.[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015488–491-231) The Reagan administration's attempts to release these hostages would be a major component of the Iran-Contra Scandal. In response to the U.S. intervention in Lebanon, the Defense Department developed the "[Powell Doctrine](/wiki/Powell_Doctrine "Powell Doctrine")", which stated that the U.S. should intervene militarily as a last resort and should set clear and limited goals in such interventions.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerring2008875-232) Though termed the Powell doctrine, the policy was originally developed by Secretary of Defense Weinberger, who was influenced not only by Lebanon but also by the experience of the Vietnam War.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005200–201-233)

#### Libya bombing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Libya bombing")]

Main article: [1986 United States bombing of Libya](/wiki/1986_United_States_bombing_of_Libya "1986 United States bombing of Libya")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/President_Reagan_and_Prime_Minister_Margaret_Thatcher_at_Camp_David_1986.jpg/250px-President_Reagan_and_Prime_Minister_Margaret_Thatcher_at_Camp_David_1986.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Reagan_and_Prime_Minister_Margaret_Thatcher_at_Camp_David_1986.jpg)

British prime minister [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") (here walking with President Reagan at [Camp David](/wiki/Camp_David "Camp David") in 1986) granted the U.S. use of British airbases to launch the Libya attack

Relations between Libya and the United States under President Reagan were continually contentious, beginning with the [Gulf of Sidra incident](/wiki/Gulf_of_Sidra_incident_(1981) "Gulf of Sidra incident (1981)") in 1981; by 1982, Libyan leader [Muammar Gaddafi](/wiki/Muammar_Gaddafi "Muammar Gaddafi") was considered by the CIA to be, along with USSR leader [Leonid Brezhnev](/wiki/Leonid_Brezhnev "Leonid Brezhnev") and Cuban leader [Fidel Castro](/wiki/Fidel_Castro "Fidel Castro"), part of a group known as the "unholy trinity" and was also labeled as "our international public enemy number one" by a CIA official.[[234]](#cite_note-iokp;-234) These tensions were later revived in early April 1986, when [a bomb exploded in a West Berlin discothèque](/wiki/1986_Berlin_discotheque_bombing "1986 Berlin discotheque bombing"), resulting in the injury of 63 American military personnel and death of one serviceman. Stating that there was irrefutable proof that Libya had directed the terrorist bombing, Reagan authorized the use of force against the country. In the late evening of April 15, 1986, the United States launched a series of [airstrikes](/wiki/Airstrike "Airstrike") on ground targets in Libya.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETucker20122102-235)[[236]](#cite_note-4-15-236)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/President_Ronald_Reagan_with_President_Hiss%C3%A8ne_Habr%C3%A9_of_Chad.jpg/250px-President_Ronald_Reagan_with_President_Hiss%C3%A8ne_Habr%C3%A9_of_Chad.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Ronald_Reagan_with_President_Hiss%C3%A8ne_Habr%C3%A9_of_Chad.jpg)

Chadian president [Hissène Habré](/wiki/Hiss%C3%A8ne_Habr%C3%A9 "Hissène Habré") at the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"). Habré was supported by the Reagan administration as an ally against Gaddafi's Libya[[237]](#cite_note-237)

Britain's prime minister, [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher"), allowed the U.S. Air Force to use Britain's air bases to launch the attack, on the justification that the UK was supporting America's right to self-defense under Article 51 of the [United Nations Charter](/wiki/United_Nations_Charter "United Nations Charter").[[236]](#cite_note-4-15-236) The attack was designed to halt Gaddafi's "ability to export terrorism", offering him "incentives and reasons to alter his criminal behavior".[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJoint_Chiefs_of_Staff2017VII-5-238) The president addressed the nation from the [Oval Office](/wiki/Oval_Office "Oval Office") after the attacks had commenced, stating, "When our citizens are attacked or abused anywhere in the world on the direct orders of hostile regimes, we will respond so long as I'm in this office."[[236]](#cite_note-4-15-236) The attack was condemned by many countries. By a vote of 79 in favor to 28 against with 33 abstentions, the [United Nations General Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly") adopted resolution 41/38 which "condemns the military attack perpetrated against the Socialist People's Libyan Arab Jamahiriya on April 15, 1986, which constitutes a violation of the Charter of the United Nations and of international law".[[239]](#cite_note-239)

### South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: South Africa")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Reagan_with_Desmond_TutuC26199-10.jpg/250px-Reagan_with_Desmond_TutuC26199-10.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reagan_with_Desmond_TutuC26199-10.jpg)

President Reagan meeting with South African anti-apartheid activist [Desmond Tutu](/wiki/Desmond_Tutu "Desmond Tutu") in 1984

During Ronald Reagan's presidency South Africa continued to use a non-democratic system of government based on racial discrimination, known as [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid"), in which the minority of white South Africans exerted nearly complete legal control over the lives of the non-white majority of the citizens. In the early 1980s the issue had moved to the center of international attention as a result of events in the townships and outcry at the death of [Stephen Biko](/wiki/Stephen_Biko "Stephen Biko"). Reagan administration policy called for "[constructive engagement](/wiki/Constructive_engagement "Constructive engagement")" with the apartheid government of South Africa. In opposition to the condemnations issued by the U.S. Congress and public demands for diplomatic or economic sanctions, Reagan made relatively minor criticisms of the regime, which was otherwise internationally isolated, and the U.S. granted recognition to the government. South Africa's military was then engaged in an occupation of [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia") and [proxy wars](/wiki/Proxy_war "Proxy war") in several neighboring countries, in alliance with Savimbi's UNITA. Reagan administration officials saw the apartheid government as a key anti-communist ally.[[240]](#cite_note-240)

Finding the Reagan Administration unresponsive to its calls for more stringent economic sanctions, anti-apartheid activists undertook a [divestment campaign](/wiki/Disinvestment_from_South_Africa "Disinvestment from South Africa"), aimed at moving individuals and institutions to sell their holdings in companies doing business in South Africa. By late 1985, facing escalating public and congressional opposition to his administration's tolerant attitude toward the South African government's policy of apartheid, Reagan made an "abrupt reversal" on the issue and proposed sanctions on the South African government, including an [arms embargo](/wiki/Arms_embargo "Arms embargo").[[241]](#cite_note-241) However, these sanctions were seen as weak by anti-apartheid activists,[[242]](#cite_note-242) and as insufficient by the president's opponents in Congress, including 81 House Republicans. In August 1986, Congress approved the [Comprehensive Anti-Apartheid Act](/wiki/Comprehensive_Anti-Apartheid_Act "Comprehensive Anti-Apartheid Act"), which included tougher sanctions. Reagan vetoed the act, but this was overridden by a bipartisan effort in Congress.[[243]](#cite_note-243) By 1990, under Reagan's successor George H. W. Bush, the new South African government of [F. W. de Klerk](/wiki/F._W._de_Klerk "F. W. de Klerk") was introducing widespread reforms, though the Bush administration argued that this was not a result of the tougher sanctions.[[244]](#cite_note-244)

### Free trade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Free trade")]

During his 1980 presidential campaign, Reagan proposed the creation of a [common market](/wiki/Single_market "Single market") in North America. Once in office, Reagan signed the [Trade and Tariff Act of 1984](/wiki/Trade_and_Tariff_Act_of_1984 "Trade and Tariff Act of 1984"), which granted the president "[fast track](/wiki/Fast_track_(trade) "Fast track (trade)")" authority in negotiating [free trade agreements](/wiki/United_States_free-trade_agreements "United States free-trade agreements").[[245]](#cite_note-kamadeo1-245) In 1985, Reagan signed the [Israel–United States Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93United_States_Free_Trade_Agreement "Israel–United States Free Trade Agreement"), the first bilateral free trade agreement in U.S. history.[[246]](#cite_note-mtolchin1-246) In 1988, Reagan and Canadian prime minister [Brian Mulroney](/wiki/Brian_Mulroney "Brian Mulroney") signed the [Canada–United States Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_Free_Trade_Agreement "Canada–United States Free Trade Agreement"), which greatly reduced trade barriers between the United States and Canada. This trade pact would serve as the foundation for the [North American Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/North_American_Free_Trade_Agreement "North American Free Trade Agreement") among the United States, Canada, and Mexico.[[245]](#cite_note-kamadeo1-245)

## Age and health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Age and health")]

At the time, Reagan was the oldest person to have served as U.S. president.[[247]](#cite_note-247) Reagan's health became a concern[*[to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] at times during his presidency.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Early in his presidency, Reagan started wearing a custom-made, technologically advanced [hearing aid](/wiki/Hearing_aid "Hearing aid"), first in his right ear[[248]](#cite_note-248) and later in his left ear as well.[[249]](#cite_note-249) His decision to go public in 1983 regarding his wearing the small, audio-amplifying device boosted their sales.[[250]](#cite_note-250)

On July 13, 1985, Reagan underwent surgery to remove 2 feet of the upper section of his colon due to a [cancer](/wiki/Colorectal_cancer "Colorectal cancer") tumor in his [cecum](/wiki/Cecum "Cecum").[[251]](#cite_note-251) He relinquished presidential power to the vice president for eight hours in a similar procedure as outlined in the [25th Amendment](/wiki/Twenty-fifth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twenty-fifth Amendment to the United States Constitution"), which he specifically avoided invoking.[[252]](#cite_note-252) The surgery lasted just under three hours and was successful.[[253]](#cite_note-253) Reagan resumed the powers of the presidency later that day.[[254]](#cite_note-254) In August of that year, he underwent an operation to remove skin cancer cells from his nose.[[255]](#cite_note-255) In October, more skin cancer cells were detected on his nose and removed.[[256]](#cite_note-256)

Former White House correspondent [Lesley Stahl](/wiki/Lesley_Stahl "Lesley Stahl") later wrote that in 1986, she and other reporters noticed what might have been early symptoms of Reagan's later [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease"). She said that on her last day on the beat, Reagan spoke to her for a few moments and did not seem to know who she was before returning to his normal behavior.[[257]](#cite_note-257) However, Reagan's primary physician, John Hutton, has said that Reagan "absolutely" did not "show any signs of dementia or Alzheimer's" during his presidency.[[258]](#cite_note-258) His doctors have noted that he began exhibiting Alzheimer's symptoms only after he left the White House.[[259]](#cite_note-259)

In January 1987, Reagan underwent surgery for an enlarged [prostate](/wiki/Prostate "Prostate") that caused further worries about his health. No cancerous growths were found, and he was not sedated during the operation.[[260]](#cite_note-260) In July of that year, he underwent a third skin cancer operation on his nose.[[261]](#cite_note-261)

On January 7, 1989, Reagan underwent surgery to repair a [Dupuytren's contracture](/wiki/Dupuytren%27s_contracture "Dupuytren's contracture") of the ring finger of his left hand.[[262]](#cite_note-262)

### 1982 mid-term elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: 1982 mid-term elections")]

Main article: [1982 United States elections](/wiki/1982_United_States_elections "1982 United States elections")

In the 1982 mid-term elections, Democrats retained a majority of the House while Republicans retained control of the Senate. Democratic gains in the House put a check on Reagan's policies, as the incoming Congress was significantly less open to Reagan's conservative policies. Despite the Democratic electoral gains, the election represented the first time since the [1930 elections](/wiki/1930_United_States_elections "1930 United States elections") that the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") successfully defended a majority in either chamber of Congress.[[263]](#cite_note-264)

### 1984 re-election campaign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: 1984 re-election campaign")]

Main articles: [Ronald Reagan 1984 presidential campaign](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_1984_presidential_campaign "Ronald Reagan 1984 presidential campaign") and [1984 United States presidential election](/wiki/1984_United_States_presidential_election "1984 United States presidential election")

Further information: [1984 United States elections](/wiki/1984_United_States_elections "1984 United States elections"), [1984 Republican Party presidential primaries](/wiki/1984_Republican_Party_presidential_primaries "1984 Republican Party presidential primaries"), and [1984 Republican National Convention](/wiki/1984_Republican_National_Convention "1984 Republican National Convention")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/ElectoralCollege1984.svg/500px-ElectoralCollege1984.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ElectoralCollege1984.svg)

President Reagan defeated Democrat [Walter Mondale](/wiki/Walter_Mondale "Walter Mondale") in the 1984 presidential election. President Reagan was reelected in one of the [largest landslide election victories in American history](/wiki/List_of_United_States_presidential_elections_by_popular_vote_margin "List of United States presidential elections by popular vote margin"), winning 59% of the popular vote, receiving 54,455,472 votes to Mondale's 37,577,352 votes. President Reagan won an even larger [Electoral College](/wiki/United_States_Electoral_College "United States Electoral College") victory, winning 525 electoral votes to 13 for Mondale.

Reagan's approval ratings fell after his first year in office, but they bounced back when the United States began to emerge from recession in 1983.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeuchtenburg2015620–621-265) The leading candidates in the [1984 Democratic presidential primaries](/wiki/1984_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "1984 Democratic Party presidential primaries") were former vice president [Walter Mondale](/wiki/Walter_Mondale "Walter Mondale"), Senator [Gary Hart](/wiki/Gary_Hart "Gary Hart") of Colorado, and African-American civil rights activist [Jesse Jackson](/wiki/Jesse_Jackson "Jesse Jackson"). Though Hart won several primaries, Mondale ultimately won the nomination. Down in the polls, Mondale [selected](/wiki/Democratic_Party_vice_presidential_candidate_selection,_1984 "Democratic Party vice presidential candidate selection, 1984") Congresswoman [Geraldine Ferraro](/wiki/Geraldine_Ferraro "Geraldine Ferraro") as his running mate in hopes of galvanizing support for his campaign, thus making Ferraro the first female major party vice presidential nominee in U.S. history.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015166–169,_173-266) In accepting the Democratic nomination, Mondale attacked Reagan's policies regarding the environment, Social Security, nuclear arms, civil rights, and other issues, stating that the Reagan administration was "of the rich, by the rich, and for the rich".[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015171–172-267) He also criticized the federal debt accumulated under Reagan, stating, "...The budget will be squeezed. Taxes will go up. And anyone who says they won't is not telling the truth to the American people."[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015452–453-94)

Reagan, meanwhile, generally declined to offer new legislative proposals for his re-election campaign, instead focusing on events like the U.S.-hosted [1984 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics") and the 40th anniversary of the [Normandy landings](/wiki/Normandy_landings "Normandy landings").[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015169–170,_176-268) Reagan's ability to perform the duties of president for another term was questioned by some observers, especially after a weak performance in the first presidential debate. His apparent confused and forgetful behavior was evident to his supporters; they had previously known him clever and witty. Rumors began to circulate that he had [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease").[[268]](#cite_note-269)[[269]](#cite_note-270) Reagan rebounded in the second debate, and confronted questions about his age, quipping, "I will not make age an issue of this campaign. I am not going to exploit, for political purposes, my opponent's youth and inexperience," which generated applause and laughter, even from Mondale himself.[[270]](#cite_note-271)

Public opinion polling consistently showed a Reagan lead in the 1984 campaign, and Mondale was unable to shake up the race.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015173-272) In the end, Reagan won re-election, winning 49 of 50 states.[[272]](#cite_note-84_results-273) Mondale carried only his home state of Minnesota and the District of Columbia. Reagan won a record 525 electoral votes,[[273]](#cite_note-pres-274) and received 59% of the popular vote to Mondale's 41%.[[272]](#cite_note-84_results-273) Compared to 1980, Reagan's strongest gains came among white Southern voters, and he also performed particularly well among Catholic voters, voters between the ages of eighteen and twenty-nine, and voters over the age of sixty.[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015173–174-275) In the concurrent [congressional elections](/wiki/1984_United_States_elections "1984 United States elections"), Republicans retained control of the [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") and Democrats retained control of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives").

### 1986 mid-term elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: 1986 mid-term elections")]

Main article: [1986 United States elections](/wiki/1986_United_States_elections "1986 United States elections")

In the 1986 mid-term elections, Democrats retained a majority of the House and won control of the Senate for the first time since the 1980 elections. Reagan campaigned hard for congressional Republicans, and an October 1986 New York Times/CBS News Poll had found that Reagan had a 67% approval rating. However, Senate Republicans faced a difficult map that year, as they had to defend 22 of the 34 seats up for election. Republican losses in the Senate were concentrated in the South and in the farm states.[[275]](#cite_note-1986midterms-276) The Republican loss of the Senate precluded the possibility of further major conservative legislation during the Reagan administration.[[276]](#cite_note-277)

### 1988 elections and transition period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: 1988 elections and transition period")]

Main article: [1988 United States elections](/wiki/1988_United_States_elections "1988 United States elections")

Further information: [George H. W. Bush 1988 presidential campaign](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush_1988_presidential_campaign "George H. W. Bush 1988 presidential campaign"), [Michael Dukakis 1988 presidential campaign](/wiki/Michael_Dukakis_1988_presidential_campaign "Michael Dukakis 1988 presidential campaign"), [1988 United States presidential election](/wiki/1988_United_States_presidential_election "1988 United States presidential election"), and [Presidential transition of George H. W. Bush](/wiki/Presidential_transition_of_George_H._W._Bush "Presidential transition of George H. W. Bush")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/ElectoralCollege1988.svg/500px-ElectoralCollege1988.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ElectoralCollege1988.svg)

Republican [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") defeated Democrat [Michael Dukakis](/wiki/Michael_Dukakis "Michael Dukakis") in the 1988 presidential election.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/President_Ronald_Reagan_Nancy_Reagan_George_Bush_Barbara_Bush_Dan_Quayle_Marilyn_Quayle_Photo_Op_with_Newly_Elected_George_Bush_and_Dan_Quayle.jpg/250px-President_Ronald_Reagan_Nancy_Reagan_George_Bush_Barbara_Bush_Dan_Quayle_Marilyn_Quayle_Photo_Op_with_Newly_Elected_George_Bush_and_Dan_Quayle.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Ronald_Reagan_Nancy_Reagan_George_Bush_Barbara_Bush_Dan_Quayle_Marilyn_Quayle_Photo_Op_with_Newly_Elected_George_Bush_and_Dan_Quayle.jpg)

Outgoing president Ronald Reagan with his wife [Nancy](/wiki/Nancy_Reagan "Nancy Reagan"), vice president and president-elect [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") with his wife [Barbara](/wiki/Barbara_Bush "Barbara Bush"), and vice president-elect [Dan Quayle](/wiki/Dan_Quayle "Dan Quayle") with his wife [Marilyn](/wiki/Marilyn_Quayle "Marilyn Quayle") in the [White House Rose Garden](/wiki/White_House_Rose_Garden "White House Rose Garden") on November 9, 1988

Reagan remained publicly neutral in the [1988 Republican presidential primaries](/wiki/1988_Republican_Party_presidential_primaries "1988 Republican Party presidential primaries"), but privately supported Vice President [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") over Senator [Bob Dole](/wiki/Bob_Dole "Bob Dole"). The [1988 Republican National Convention](/wiki/1988_Republican_National_Convention "1988 Republican National Convention"), which nominated Bush for president, also acted as a celebration of Reagan's presidency.[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrands2015697–698-278) Democrats nominated [Michael Dukakis](/wiki/Michael_Dukakis "Michael Dukakis"), the liberal Governor of Massachusetts. Following the [1988 Democratic National Convention](/wiki/1988_Democratic_National_Convention "1988 Democratic National Convention"), Dukakis led the polls by seventeen points, but Bush, aided by the INF Treaty and the strong economy, closed the gap as the election neared. Democrats tried to link Bush to the Iran–Contra Scandal, but Bush claimed that he had not been involved. The GOP effectively cast Dukakis as "soft" on crime and foreign policy issues, seizing on Dukakis's pardon of [Willie Horton](/wiki/Willie_Horton "Willie Horton") and his dispassionate response to a question regarding the death penalty.

In the [1988 presidential election](/wiki/1988_United_States_presidential_election "1988 United States presidential election"), Bush soundly defeated Dukakis, taking 53.4 percent of the popular vote and 426 electoral votes.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005220–225-279) The election saw the lowest turnout of eligible voters in any presidential election since [1948](/wiki/1948_United_States_presidential_election "1948 United States presidential election").[[279]](#cite_note-280) In the concurrent [congressional elections](/wiki/1988_United_States_elections "1988 United States elections"), Democrats retained control of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") and the [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate").[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005220–225-279) In large part due to his handling of relations with the Soviet Union, Reagan left office with an approval rating of sixty-eight percent,[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005217-281) matching the approval ratings of Franklin Roosevelt and later [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") as the highest rating for a departing president in the modern era.[[281]](#cite_note-282)

## Evaluation and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Evaluation and legacy")]

Main articles: [Opinion polling on the Reagan administration](/wiki/Opinion_polling_on_the_Reagan_administration "Opinion polling on the Reagan administration") and [United States presidential approval rating](/wiki/United_States_presidential_approval_rating "United States presidential approval rating")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Gallup_Poll-Approval_Rating-Ronald_Reagan.png/500px-Gallup_Poll-Approval_Rating-Ronald_Reagan.png)](/wiki/File:Gallup_Poll-Approval_Rating-Ronald_Reagan.png)

Graph of Reagan's approval ratings in [Gallup](/wiki/Gallup_(company) "Gallup (company)") polls

Since Reagan left office in 1989, substantial debate has occurred among scholars, historians, and the general public surrounding his legacy.[[282]](#cite_note-283) Supporters have pointed to a more efficient and prosperous economy as a result of Reagan's economic policies,[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayward2009635–638-284) foreign policy triumphs including a peaceful end to the Cold War,[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeschloss2007324-285) and a restoration of American pride and morale.[[285]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECannon2000746-286) Proponents also argue Reagan restored faith in the American Dream[[286]](#cite_note-287) after a decline in American confidence and self-respect under Jimmy Carter's perceived weak leadership, particularly during the [Iran hostage crisis](/wiki/Iran_hostage_crisis "Iran hostage crisis").[[287]](#cite_note-288) Reagan remains an important symbol of American conservatism, much in the same way that Franklin Roosevelt continued to serve as a symbol of liberalism long after his own death.[[288]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossinow2015293-289)

Critics contend that Reagan's economic policies resulted in rising budget deficits,[[289]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECannonBeschloss2001128-290) a wider [gap in wealth](/wiki/Wealth_inequality_in_the_United_States "Wealth inequality in the United States"), and an increase in [homelessness](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness").[[290]](#cite_note-291) Liberals especially disapproved of Reagan's simultaneous tax cuts for the wealthy and benefit cuts for the poor.[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005158-292) Some critics assert that the Iran–Contra affair lowered American credibility.[[292]](#cite_note-293) In his popular book, *The Rise and Fall of the Great Powers*, historian [Paul Kennedy](/wiki/Paul_Kennedy "Paul Kennedy") argued that Reagan's high level of defense would eventually lead to the decline of the United States as a [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power").[[293]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005202-294) Reagan's leadership and understanding of issues has also been questioned, and even some members of the administration criticized Reagan's passive demeanor during meetings with staff and cabinet members.[[294]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson2005160–161-295) [Richard Pipes](/wiki/Richard_Pipes "Richard Pipes"), a member of the National Security Council, criticized Reagan as "really lost, out of his depth, uncomfortable" at NSC meetings.[[295]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeffler2007349-296) Another NSC member, [Colin Powell](/wiki/Colin_Powell "Colin Powell"), criticized Reagan's "passive management style [that] placed a tremendous burden on us".[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPemberton1997151-297)

Despite the continuing debate surrounding his legacy, many conservative and liberal scholars agree that Reagan has been one of the most influential presidents since Franklin Roosevelt, leaving his imprint on American politics, diplomacy, culture, and economics through his effective communication, dedicated patriotism, and pragmatic compromising.[[297]](#cite_note-298) Since he left office, historians have reached a consensus,[[298]](#cite_note-299) as summarized by British historian M. J. Heale, who finds that scholars now concur that Reagan rehabilitated conservatism, turned the nation to the right, practiced a considerably pragmatic conservatism that balanced ideology and the constraints of politics, revived faith in the presidency and in [American exceptionalism](/wiki/American_exceptionalism "American exceptionalism"), and contributed to victory in the Cold War.[[299]](#cite_note-300) [Hugh Heclo](/wiki/Hugh_Heclo "Hugh Heclo") argues that Reagan himself failed to roll back the [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state"), but that he contributed to a shift in attitudes that led to the defeat of efforts to further expand the welfare state.[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeclo2008558–560-301) Heclo further argues that Reagan's presidency made American voters and political leaders more tolerant of deficits and more opposed to taxation.[[301]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeclo2008562–563-302)

In 2011 a [public opinion](/wiki/Public_opinion "Public opinion") survey by Gallup found Reagan was the top-ranked President, even beating out Lincoln.[[302]](#cite_note-303) In 2017, a C-SPAN survey of scholars ranked Reagan as the ninth greatest president.[[303]](#cite_note-304)[[304]](#cite_note-305) A 2018 poll of the [American Political Science Association](/wiki/American_Political_Science_Association "American Political Science Association")'s presidents and Executive Politics section also ranked Reagan as the ninth greatest president.[[305]](#cite_note-306) In terms of the worst mistake made by a sitting president, a 2006 poll of historians ranked the Iran-Contra affair as the ninth worst.[[306]](#cite_note-307)

### Historiography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Historiography")]

* Johns, Andrew L., ed. *A Companion to Ronald Reagan* (Wiley-Blackwell, 2015). xiv, 682 pp.; topical essays by scholars emphasizing historiography; [contents free at many libraries](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/book/10.1002/9781118607770)
* Kengor, Paul. ["Reagan Among the Professors: His Surprising Reputation"](https://go.gale.com/ps/i.do?id=GALE%7CA60037190&sid=googleScholar&v=2.1&it=r&linkaccess=abs&issn=01465945&p=AONE&sw=w&userGroupName=mlin_oweb&isGeoAuthType=true). *Policy Review* 98 (1999): 15+. Reports that "many articles in the top journals have been fair, as have a number of influential books...from respected historians, presidential scholars, and political scientists – people who were not Reagan supporters and are certainly not right-wingers."
